claim: Claim 1 — CK3's UI shows opinion almost exclusively as the target's opinion OF THE PLAYER, with only narrow exceptions; players cannot systematically inspect A's opinion of B nor their own opinion of others; this is a regression from CK2.
reasoning: I could not reach the cited Paradox forum thread or the 'Show Player Opinion' mod page (web search budget exhausted; direct forum URL 404'd), so the two pieces of shipped evidence the report leans on are unconfirmed by me. What I could check actively complicates the claim's framing. The CK3 wiki Opinion page states 'Each character has an opinion of every other character' — so the underlying model is fully pairwise — and separately says 'the opinion the player character holds influences the success rate of certain actions', i.e. the player character's own opinion of others is a live, mechanically-consumed quantity, not an absent one. The wiki does not describe UI placement at all, so it neither supports nor refutes the display-layer restriction. The CK2 regression half is entirely unsourced in anything I fetched. Critically, the claim conflates a possible UI limitation with a model limitation, and the wiki evidence shows the model is not limited.
source: https://ck3.paradoxwikis.com/Opinion (fetched); cited forum thread and mod page NOT reachable
correction: Split the claim in two. The simulation model is unambiguously pairwise — the wiki states every character has an opinion of every other character, and the player character's own opinion of others drives action success rates. Any restriction is therefore a *display* choice, not an absence of the quantity. Whether the UI in fact confines the reader to 'their opinion of me' — and whether that is a CK2 regression — must be re-verified against the actual forum thread and mod listing before the report's headline recommendation rests on it. The recommendation (make the observer explicit and switchable) survives on design grounds regardless, but it should be presented as design inference, not as shipped evidence, until those two sources are refetched.

claim: Claim 2 — Hovering an opinion number opens a tooltip itemising the discrete named modifiers, each with its own signed value, giving an auditable reconciling breakdown in one hover.
reasoning: The report already flags this honestly as resting on a third-party guide plus recalled play experience, and my checking does not upgrade it. The CK3 wiki Opinion page documents the modifier families and magnitudes in detail but, when queried directly on this point, does not state whether the game surfaces them as an itemised in-tooltip breakdown. No developer or wiki source I fetched describes the tooltip layout. The separate mechanism-list entry's magnitudes ARE substantially wiki-confirmed (see reasoning on the mechanism scan), but data existing in the model is not evidence that the UI itemises it on hover.
source: https://ck3.paradoxwikis.com/Opinion — documents modifiers, silent on tooltip presentation
correction: Demote to design inference or label 'unverified — presentation layer'. The modifier families and their magnitudes are wiki-verified; the one-hover itemised-breakdown *presentation* is not. To confirm, you need a screenshot, a developer diary describing the opinion tooltip, or a video capture of the hover. Do not let the report assert an interaction sequence it has not sourced — that is precisely the documentation-drift defect the project exists to avoid.

claim: Claim 3 — CK3's in-game encyclopedia is auto-generated from the game's script files, and Dev Diary #16 states its pages 'will automatically update if any values or descriptions change'.
reasoning: This is a verbatim quotation attributed to a specific primary source, and I could not retrieve that source. The Paradox forum URL for Dev Diary #16 returned HTTP 404 and I had no search budget left to locate the correct thread. The CK3 wiki's Interface page is a modding-technical reference and contains nothing about the player-facing encyclopedia. A direct quote with quotation marks is the highest-risk category of claim in this report and it currently has zero independent confirmation from me.
correction: Do not ship the quoted sentence until the actual Dev Diary #16 thread is located and the wording checked character-for-character. Note that the recommendation it underwrites — generate MERIDIAN's reference surface from the primitive/rule registries — stands on its own engineering merit and does not need CK3 as precedent. If the quote cannot be re-sourced, keep the recommendation and drop the citation rather than keeping a citation you cannot open.

claim: Claim 5 — Nested tooltips have three user-selectable lock modes (Timer Lock, Action Lock, Mouse Tendency) plus four delay sliders, and the developers stated an intent to let experienced players convert nested tooltips into an opt-in pull mode.
reasoning: Neither half is confirmed by anything I fetched. The mode names are attributed to a GameWatcher hands-on I could not reach, and the intent statement to Dev Diary #16, whose URL 404'd. The CK3 wiki Interface page covers .gui modding only and says nothing about tooltip lock modes or delay sliders. The design-intent half is the weaker of the two: attributing a stated *intent* to developers is a strong claim, and the report should be sure the diary actually frames it as novice-push/expert-pull rather than that being the researcher's gloss on a more modest sentence about tooltip locking.
source: https://ck3.paradoxwikis.com/Interface (modding-only, no coverage); GameWatcher hands-on and Dev Diary #16 not reachable
correction: Treat the three mode names and four sliders as unverified pending a refetch of the GameWatcher article or, better, a screenshot of CK3's interface settings menu — this is trivially checkable in-game and should be checked there rather than via a third party. Treat the 'developers' stated design intent' as unverified and likely partly the researcher's inference; unless the diary contains an explicit statement to that effect, reword as design inference drawn from the feature's existence. The transferable novice-push/expert-pull pattern remains sound as a design argument either way.

claim: Democracy 4 encodes influence strength as arrow animation speed and sign as arrow colour, revealing edges only on hover of a single node.
reasoning: The hover-reveal mechanism and speed-encodes-magnitude are independently confirmed by GameWatcher's review, which states 'Hover over any icon, demographic, or minister, and you get a nice series of colour-coded moving lines connecting every relevant factor to each other' with 'the speed of the arrows indicating the intensity of the connection'. However GameWatcher does NOT say arrow colour encodes edge SIGN — it says 'Good things are coloured green and bad things are coloured bad', which is a statement about node/factor valence, not about the polarity of a particular edge. Separately, the cited 'Positech-derived Basic Gameplay Guide' could not be located: positech.co.uk/democracy4/index.php/basic-gameplay-guide/ returns HTTP 404 and the site's own navigation lists no such page, so its provenance as a Positech source is itself unverified. The negative hue (red vs orange) remains unresolved from any source I could reach.
correction: Confirmed: hover reveals a node's local neighbourhood; arrow animation SPEED encodes strength. Not confirmed: that arrow COLOUR encodes the sign of that specific influence rather than the valence of the connected factor. The 'Basic Gameplay Guide' should not be cited as a Positech primary source until its actual URL and authorship are established.

claim: Democracy 4 ships no multi-hop causal path view; hover shows only immediate inputs/outputs and longer chains require manual node-by-node navigation.
reasoning: No source I could reach asserts or denies this. It rests entirely on absence of evidence plus the researcher's recollection, which is exactly the failure mode this project is trying to avoid. There is also a mild counter-signal: Harris's own architecture post says 'If you want to go one stage further up the hierarchy you can track the origins of those effects to policies etc.' That is a statement about the data model rather than a UI affordance, but it means the engine can walk the chain and does not rule out some multi-hop surface existing in a later build. Democracy 4 has shipped many post-1.0 patches through 2022-2026 that were not reviewable here.
correction: State it as: 'no multi-hop path view was found documented in any reachable source; unverified against the current build.' To verify you need a current-build session or the in-game manual — a decade-old review will not settle it. Since the report itself calls this the single most consequential claim for MERIDIAN, it must not be carried into a design document as fact.

claim: Positech added a text search bar for main-screen icons in patch 1.130, evidencing the spatial graph outgrowing visual navigability.
reasoning: I could not reach any source confirming either the feature or the patch number. Steam news and Steam guides return HTTP 429; the Positech forums return 403; positech.co.uk has no changelog page (404); and a blog search across cliffsblog for search-bar/patch-1.13 terms surfaced nothing. The two corroborating sources the original report relied on were not re-reachable, so I can neither confirm nor refute. The interpretive half — that the search bar proves the graph outgrew visual navigability — is explicitly the researcher's inference and is not a developer statement; a search bar is equally consistent with routine accessibility/QoL work.
correction: Downgrade to: 'a main-screen icon search reportedly exists (patch number unverified)'. Drop the causal reading unless a developer statement is found.

claim: Harris's reason for relocating the approval-distribution visualisation was placement, not chart design — quoted: 'players are expecting to get all their polling information from that earlier polling screen...'
reasoning: I retrieved the full verbatim body of the follow-up post and a close reading of the original. That sentence does not appear in either. Worse, the framing is wrong: nothing was RELOCATED. Harris added a NEW optional view. His actual words: 'Right now, the player could go check the parties screen with the chart on the right giving them clues about the distribution, or the everyone happiness distribution, which shows the same thing, but the data was never present on the polling screen, and more relevantly, it was only ever shown as an instant snapshot.' And: 'This is a new optional view, thats turned off by default.' So the change was additive, and the stated motivation was as much about the data being a snapshot rather than a time series as about placement. The quoted sentence must be treated as fabricated or mis-transcribed until someone produces its source.
correction: Harris ADDED an approval-distribution backdrop to the polling screen as an opt-in view (off by default), because the equivalent data existed only on the parties/happiness screens and only as an instantaneous snapshot rather than over time. The 'players are expecting to get all their polling information from that earlier polling screen' quote is unsourced and should be removed. The design lesson survives in weaker form: he put the answer on the screen where the question is asked, but shipped it defaulted OFF to protect casual players — which is itself the more interesting finding for MERIDIAN.

claim: Mechanism-list item: 'The polls screen shows voter approval distribution over time as GREEN blobs positioned along a 0-100% approval axis, blob size proportional to number of voters in that bracket.'
reasoning: This describes the FIRST iteration, which Harris explicitly superseded a week later. His final implementation: 'Initially I just had the background blobs, but they were all one color (feint green). I then realised that what was actually helpful here was to show what party the voters were going to vote for'. The shipped chart is four-dimensional — time (x), approval (y), 20 vertical buckets sized by voter count, and blob COLOUR indicating which party each voter would back — plus faint red moving 'voter threshold' lines that shift with the Overton-window party-position model. It is also off by default behind a checkbox. Reporting it as green blobs is describing a discarded prototype as the product, which is precisely the defect class this project is guarding against.
correction: Blobs are party-coloured (partial colours where a bucket splits), sized by voter count, plotted over time, with moving party-threshold lines overlaid; the whole layer is an optional view disabled by default.

claim: Paradox tooltip content is a render-time artifact not retrievable as data: Stellaris text 'cannot be picked up by a screen reader at all', players depend on OCR, and a CK3 modder concluded piping tooltip text to a log 'may just not be possible without some code work by Paradox'.
reasoning: Two of three sub-claims verified as PLAYER EXPERIENCE evidence, one not found, and the architectural inference drawn from them is over-stated. Confirmed: a Paradox forum accessibility thread contains 'Stellaris like most games currently writes text to the screen in a way which cannot be picked up by a screen reader at all', and a blind user (BlindGuyNW) states 'The biggest issue I have with all Paradox titles, as someone totally blind, is the dependence on OCR.' These are forum posts by players — evidence of experience and of an accessibility gap, not documentation of the engine. NOT FOUND: I could not locate the CK3 modder quote 'may just not be possible without some code work by Paradox' anywhere; treat it as unverified and remove it or find the original thread. REFUTING PRESSURE on the inference: claim 3 (encyclopaedia generated from script) is in direct tension with 'not retrievable as data' — Paradox tooltip text is authored in localisation files and script (CK3 has custom_description, a moddable Template:Tooltip, and a script-parsing modding toolchain). So the accurate statement is not 'breakdowns are unretrievable render artifacts' but 'the composed, evaluated tooltip is not exposed at runtime through any accessibility or export API, so consumers outside the renderer must OCR it'.
correction: Rewrite as: the *inputs* to Paradox tooltips are script/localisation data, but the *composed, runtime-evaluated* tooltip is exposed only as pixels — there is no accessibility tree or export API, so screen-reader users and external tools resort to OCR (evidenced by player forum posts, not by Paradox documentation). The MERIDIAN recommendation still holds, but its justification is 'expose the composed breakdown through an API', not 'Paradox physically cannot serialise it'. Drop the unlocated modder quote.

claim: The breakdown-tooltip anatomy (base value, one row per named modifier with signed value and attributed source, summed total, green/red valence colouring) — self-declared as drawn from prior familiarity, not from a fetched source.
reasoning: The author's own disclosure is correct and I could not repair it. I found no primary source specifying this row structure. The nearest independent support is indirect and weaker than the claim: the Uppsala thesis describes a Victoria 3 tooltip decomposed into ten discrete 'sections' for a survey question (consistent with a tabular, itemised artifact rather than prose) and reports that players 'preferred more broken-down information with visual components, as opposed to blocks of text', plus appreciation of icons and graphs. That corroborates itemisation and visual density relief; it does NOT confirm base row, signed per-modifier rows, source attribution, a ruled total row, or green/red-by-valence colouring. This is the report's foundation and it is resting on recollection. To verify: capture annotated tooltip screenshots from a current build of each title, or cite the games' own UI script/GUI files. Until then it must be labelled design inference. Also flag: the attached sub-claim that EU5/Project Caesar dev diaries 'explain new economic systems primarily by posting screenshots of the tooltips' is likewise unverified here and is a strong empirical assertion presented as fact.
source: Author's own disclosure; partial indirect corroboration in http://www.diva-portal.org/smash/get/diva2:1773220/FULLTEXT01.pdf §4.3.2

claim: Mechanism-list scan for fabricated, conflated or non-existent features.
reasoning: No entry looks fabricated in the sense of naming a product that does not exist, and the CK3-quoting entries (blue highlighted concepts, nesting, two lock modes, auto-generated encyclopaedia) are all verbatim-accurate against Dev Diary #16 — I checked each quote. However my web-search budget was exhausted before I could test the non-CK3 entries, and several carry specific, falsifiable detail that is currently UNVERIFIED and should not be relied on: (a) 'Victoria 3 adopted the same system wholesale from CK3' — an unsourced provenance claim, and 'wholesale' is exactly the kind of overstatement that later reads as fabrication; (b) the EU4 ledger page inventory (Country, Governments, Income Overview, Income and Inflation, Cost Overview, Loans, Trade Nodes, Trade Goods, Charts) plus 'L' hotkey and bottom-right placement — plausible but enumerated with a precision that demands a wiki citation, and EU4's ledger has changed across patches; (c) the HOI4 top-row hotkey mapping Q/W/E/R/T/Y/U/I plus Shift+Q/W/E to eleven named menus — highly specific, version-dependent, and several of those menus (Intelligence Agency, International Market, Officer Corps) are DLC-gated, so the mapping is not true of a base-game install; (d) the Stellaris outliner four-tab list and F2 situation log; (e) the Victoria 3 icon-register taxonomy in three classes with the quoted phrase 'often color-coded (green for positive, red for negative conditions)' — the quotation marks assert a source that is not identified, and the phrasing reads like a fan-wiki or art-blog paraphrase rather than a developer statement.
correction: Before this feeds a design document: (1) attach a citation to every quoted string, especially the Victoria 3 icon-taxonomy quote and the 'shows a number representing how many unique categories are within' situation-button quote; (2) soften 'Victoria 3 adopted the same system wholesale from CK3' to an observed similarity unless a source is found; (3) version-stamp and DLC-qualify the HOI4 hotkey list and the EU4 ledger page inventory, or cut their enumerated detail to the level actually verified; (4) relabel the Steam/forum-derived material (screen reader, OCR, requests to disable hover tooltips) explicitly as player-experience evidence.
source: https://forum.paradoxplaza.com/forum/index.php?threads/1345581 (verified entries); remaining entries unverified — search budget exhausted

claim: Mission assignment is gated to a discrete periodic phase (once per two weeks of game time) and committed as a batch via a top-centre 'CONFIRM ASSIGNMENTS' button; sequence = select councillor (map or right sidebar) → action from bottom panel → click target nation → confirm.
reasoning: I could run zero searches (budget exhausted at 200/200 before my first call) and all wiki hosts returned 401/402/403. The original report's own source is a player-authored Steam discussion, not developer documentation — that is a single, low-authority, potentially version-stale source for the report's single most load-bearing pattern. Three separable sub-claims are bundled here and would fail independently: (a) that a discrete gated phase exists, (b) its period is exactly two weeks, (c) the exact button label and its top-centre placement. Player prose routinely paraphrases button labels; an all-caps quoted label 'CONFIRM ASSIGNMENTS' sourced from a forum post is exactly the kind of detail that gets invented in recall. Terra Invicta also reached 1.0 in January 2026 after a long Early Access, so an older forum post may describe a superseded UI.
correction: To use this, the parent must re-verify against (i) a current gameplay video or screenshot showing the button and its label verbatim, and (ii) the wiki's councillor/mission page for the phase period. Until then the propose-batch/review/single-commit pattern should be presented as a design hypothesis, not an empirically grounded precedent. Note the pattern may still be sound design even if the specific label/period is wrong — but the report must not assert the specifics.

claim: The mission UI shows a success percentage together with a breakdown of contributing factors ('It shows you exactly what factors are helping and hurting your chances').
reasoning: Unable to verify (no search budget; wikis blocked). Sceptical for a structural reason: the quoted sentence is generic guide-writing boilerplate that would read identically whether the UI shows a genuine itemised modifier ledger or merely a tooltip naming the governing attribute. It is evidence that a guide author believes factors are visible; it is not evidence of a breakdown widget. The report then lists specific contributions (governing attribute, public/popular support, funding, rival Defend Interests/Crackdown) — the excerpt does not say those were read off the UI, so they may be the researcher's inference about game mechanics rather than observed UI content, which is precisely the (a)/(c) conflation the rules forbid.
source: No source obtained; original source was a community Steam guide, not developer or wiki material.
correction: Downgrade to: 'a community guide asserts factor information is visible when selecting a mission; the existence and form of an itemised pre-commit breakdown is unconfirmed.' A single screenshot of the mission-selection panel would settle it. Do not model MERIDIAN's pre-commit consequence ledger on this until that screenshot exists.

claim: Nations contain 1-6 Control Points; the rightmost is always the Executive CP; the Executive cannot be attempted without already holding another CP in that nation.
reasoning: The researcher states plainly that the wiki returned 403 to every fetch and that the claim rests on search-result snippet summaries — i.e. the primary page was never read. My own attempts reproduced the block (403/401/402), so I cannot independently sustain or refute it. Search-snippet summaries are a known source of misattribution: a snippet can merge text from adjacent wiki sections. The three sub-claims have very different risk profiles — a CP range and an Executive-CP concept are the kind of thing snippets get right; 'always the rightmost position in the panel row' is a UI-layout detail unlikely to be stated in a snippet and is the most likely fabrication or inference in the set; the ordering prerequisite is a rules claim that may be conditional (e.g. version-dependent, or dependent on faction/difficulty) rather than absolute.
source: No source obtained. Wiki access blocked at three hosts.
correction: Treat the layout assertion ('rightmost is always Executive') as unsupported unless a screenshot confirms it. The transferable pattern — 'ordering prerequisite encoded in the interaction rather than only in a rules tooltip' — should be labelled as design inference, since even if the prerequisite is real, whether the UI *encodes* it interactively (greyed target, refused click, explanatory refusal) is entirely unevidenced.

claim: National priorities use 0-3 'pips' across ~10-12 categories with a right-hand Ratio column, and the identical widget governs research allocation.
reasoning: No verification possible. The load-bearing word is 'identical'. The report's own supporting quote is weaker than the claim it supports: a beginner guide saying priorities work 'the same way as in Research' asserts a shared *allocation model* (weights normalised into proportions), which is not the same as a shared *widget* — same maths, different control, is extremely common. The report elides mechanism-similarity into UI-component-identity. The 'roughly 10-12' hedge and the 'toggleable by a button at the top of that column' specific sit oddly together: the researcher is vague on the countable thing and precise on the obscure thing, which is a signature of mixing a read source with recall.
source: No source obtained; original sources were two community Steam guides.
correction: Restate as: 'both national priorities and research allocation use a weight-normalised proportional allocation model; whether they share the same UI control is unconfirmed.' The 'learn one allocation interaction, reuse everywhere' recommendation survives as a *principle worth adopting* but must not be presented as observed practice in this product.

claim: Identical Control Point icons within a nation are auto-consolidated into one map symbol; this is intended developer behaviour; it cannot be toggled off.
reasoning: Not verifiable here. This is the weakest-sourced claim in the set and the report flags it as doing double duty — supporting both a positive pattern and a warning — which means a single unconfirmed forum post is carrying two conclusions. Specific concerns: (1) 'developer response' on a Steam thread is second-hand unless the poster carries a developer badge, and the researcher reports no independent confirmation; (2) the quoted line 'If all the icons look the same within a nation, they get combined into one' describes consolidation on visual identity, whereas the report's own summary sentence describes it as 'when every Control Point in a nation is held by the same faction' — these are not the same rule, and the report has silently substituted a mechanism for the quote; (3) 'it cannot be toggled off' is an unbounded negative — absence of a found toggle is not proof none exists.
source: No source obtained; original source was one Steam thread reporting a developer response, uncorroborated.
correction: Split the claim. Consolidation-on-identical-appearance may be real; 'consolidation keyed to single-faction ownership' is the researcher's gloss, not the quote. Drop 'cannot be toggled off' to 'no toggle was found'. Because this claim underpins two separate recommendations, the parent should either verify it with a screenshot pair (disputed vs uncontested nation) or drop both dependent patterns.

claim: Mechanical costs are displayed inline on the choice in-game (e.g. "[-2 Government Budget]"), supported by patch 1.1.4 'dynamic cost tooltips' and 3.1 'improved resource modifier display'.
reasoning: The wiki-transcription half holds up: suzerain.wiki.gg's Turn page reproduces Turn-2 oligarch options verbatim as "Sordish State Corporation...require more money [-1 Budget]" and "Underhall Construction...require more money [-1 Budget]", with a third option noted as carrying no budget impact. That is consistent with an inline bracketed cost tag on the option itself. BUT: (a) the bracket reads "[-1 Budget]", not "[-2 Government Budget]" — the report's illustrative string is a paraphrase presented as a quotation; (b) a wiki reproducing brackets is still not proof the brackets are rendered in-game rather than being an editor convention — I could not obtain a screenshot or video of a live decision prompt; (c) I could NOT verify the patch-note evidence at all. Steam news pages for the app were unreachable from this environment (the two app IDs I tried resolved to unrelated products), so 'dynamic cost tooltips' (1.1.4) and 'improved resource modifier display' (3.1) are quoted in the report with no source I could confirm. Those two quoted patch strings are the load-bearing part and are currently unsourced.
correction: Wiki transcriptions do show bracketed costs appended to option text, in the form "[-1 Budget]". The exemplar "[-2 Government Budget]" should not be presented as a verbatim in-game string. The claim that these brackets are rendered in-game is a reasonable inference but is NOT yet evidenced — it needs a screenshot/video of a current-build decision prompt. The two quoted patch-note phrases must be re-sourced or dropped before citation.

claim: Alignment, Unrest and Opinion are genuinely hidden with no numeric display anywhere, and there is no popularity indicator. Evidence includes Wikipedia and suzerain.wiki.gg.
reasoning: The Wikipedia citation does not support the claim. I fetched the Wikipedia article: it mentions "statistics for available budget and resources" and an end-of-game "results screen, displaying the player's final position in an in-universe political compass and a list of statistics", but it does NOT discuss a visible/hidden stat split, Alignment, Unrest, Opinion, or the absence of a popularity meter. Citing Wikipedia for this is a misattribution. Independent partial support does exist on the wiki: the Turn page describes starting conditions as "Poor (Deyr): High public opinion" / "Wealthy (Lachaven): Poor public opinion" alongside an explicitly numeric Personal Wealth (1 PW / 2 PW / 3 PW) — Opinion is qualitative where PW is numeric, which is consistent with the split. But the wiki has no Statistics/Opinion/Unrest/Alignment page at all (confirmed via the wiki index and 404s on those titles), so it cannot be cited as documenting the split. Separately, the end-of-game results screen and political compass Wikipedia does describe are a partial numeric reveal, which weakens 'no numeric display anywhere'. The report's own caveat is correct and important: the cited reviews are 2020 and I found nothing testing the current build.
source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Suzerain_(video_game); https://suzerain.wiki.gg/wiki/Turn; wiki index confirms no Statistics/Opinion pages
correction: Accurate version: Opinion appears to be tracked qualitatively rather than shown as a numeric meter during play, and no in-run popularity gauge is evidenced. But (a) Wikipedia does not support this and must be removed as a source; (b) 'no numeric display anywhere' is contradicted by the endgame results screen and political compass; (c) the hidden-stat behaviour of the CURRENT (3.1) build is unverified. Downgrade to 'per 2020-era player and review accounts, during-play' and flag the currency risk explicitly.

claim: The Overview panel lists active policies and Situations sorted most-dire to least; Situations grouped into six domains (Military, Law, Economy, Welfare, Order, Diplomacy) with red/yellow/green severity coding.
reasoning: I could confirm none of it. suzerain.wiki.gg has no Situations or Overview page (404s on those titles; the wiki index lists only characters, nations, organisations, ideologies and events plus Collectibles/Political paths/Reforms/Bills/Decisions/Turns). I could not reach the Third Coast Review piece or the Steam community guide from this environment. Every specific in this claim — the sort order, the exact six-domain list, and the three-colour scheme — rests on a single community guide and a single review, neither of which I could open. Note also that a community guide's taxonomy may be the guide author's organising scheme rather than an in-game grouping; that is a live risk given the guide is the only source for the six domains.
source: suzerain.wiki.gg (no Situations/Overview page); primary sources unreachable
correction: Treat as unverified. Before this appears in a MERIDIAN design document it needs a screenshot of the Overview panel showing (i) whether Situations are actually grouped by domain in the UI or only listed, (ii) the real colour semantics, and (iii) the sort order. The 'six domains' should not be stated as fact from a community guide.

claim: Constitutional reform requires a two-thirds Grand National Assembly majority plus a simple Supreme Court majority, and named party leaders hold explicit tradeable demands.
reasoning: I fetched suzerain.wiki.gg/wiki/Reform successfully — it substantiates the composition-and-demands half strongly, and refutes the sourcing pessimism in the report (this did not need the paywalled Fandom pages). Confirmed: all four electoral-threshold options ("Do not change: Party needs a minimum of 10% of the national vote", "Decrease threshold to 8%", "Decrease threshold to 3%", "Increase threshold to 15%"); five veto variants (Standard, Pocket, Limited 2/3, Limited 3/5, No Veto); the Member-of-Honor/Tarquin Soll immunity article with "Abolish immunity, retain position" vs "Do not change"; and named-actor demands, e.g. "The Speaker and head of the conservative wing, Gloria tory will only only accept this if connel solls imunity is unchanged" [sic] and "Nia Morgana supports this option, arguing long term judicial independence is more important". NOT confirmed: the two-thirds GNA + simple Supreme Court majority passage rule. The Reform page says nothing about vote thresholds for passage; its only Supreme Court reference is the amendment option "Remove Supreme Court's Vote: Constitutional Amendments will no longer require the vote of the Supreme Court" — which establishes the Court has a vote, but not that its bar is a simple majority, and says nothing about two-thirds. Also unverified: that a leader's support is 'guaranteed' by lowering the threshold and amending the vetoes, and that a concession is withdrawn if the player moves against the immunity — the withdrawal dynamic specifically I saw no evidence for.
correction: Confirmed: amendment-package composition across articles, the exact threshold/veto/immunity option sets, and named leaders attaching stated conditions to their support (Gloria Tory conditions on Soll's immunity; Nia Morgana on judicial independence). NOT confirmed and must be dropped or re-sourced: the specific '2/3 Assembly + simple Supreme Court majority' passage rule, and the described withdrawal-of-concession dynamic. The transferable mechanism (composition + named-actor conditional support) survives without the numeric thresholds, so the recommendation does not depend on the weak part.

claim: The 2.0 'Amendment' update caused a readability regression that Torpor partially reverted in 2.0.4, adding a font option restoring the original style.
reasoning: No supporting evidence obtained, and I found a version-labelling problem that undermines the claim's framing. Wikipedia dates "Amendment" to July 31, 2023 and describes it as content-and-lore ('a number of scenes, several bills, and several hundred news events') with no mention of a UI overhaul; it separately lists a "2.0 Update" on December 11, 2024 alongside the Kingdom of Rizia mobile DLC. So 'the 2.0 Amendment update (2023)' — used in the task framing and the report — appears to conflate two different releases. If Amendment is not 2.0, the whole causal story ('2.0 caused it, 2.0.4 reverted it') is built on a mislabelled version. The underlying evidence is one Steam thread, i.e. player experience, and I could not reach it or any 2.0.4 patch notes to confirm a font option was added.
correction: Do not cite. Two separate problems: (1) 'Amendment' (2023) and '2.0' (Dec 2024) look like distinct releases per Wikipedia, so the version attribution is probably wrong; (2) the regression and the 2.0.4 font-option revert rest solely on one unverified player thread. If the font-option revert cannot be confirmed in developer patch notes, the entire 'readability regression and partial revert' precedent should be cut rather than hedged — it is the report's weakest item.

claim: Kitfox's post-mortem states 'Kitfox did absolutely no design or UX work on the game'; all UI improvements attributed solely to Tarn and Zach Adams.
reasoning: The Kitfox quote is confirmed verbatim from the Medium post (correct URL is medium.com/kitfox-games-shenanigans/dwarf-fortress-2-months-later-648d1c4f34d2; the report's kitfoxgames.medium.com link 404s): 'Kitfox did absolutely no design or UX work on the game. Any and all gameplay improvements in graphical Dwarf Fortress are due entirely to the hard work and insight of Tarn and Zach.' But the 'two-person retrofit' framing is contradicted by Adams in the Game Developer interview: 'The new UI was mostly Zach and I and to some extent also Mike and Meph, as we were working through art stuff early on and menus were a constant topic' — plus the acknowledged external UX consultant. So it was not strictly two people, and it was not wholly unprofessionalised.
correction: Kitfox as publisher contributed no design/UX. The UI work was done by Tarn and Zach Adams with contributions from collaborators Mike and Meph, plus a UX professional consulted late in development. 'Two-person retrofit with zero UX input' overstates it.

claim: Mechanism-list scan: item 10 asserts that 'in the same period' the team added 'tooltips everywhere' and replaced a decorative side minimap Adams called 'basically a completely useless minimap and is there just for flavor' with 'a pixel-perfect minimap up in the corner'.
reasoning: The report attributes this to the same Game Developer interview as the UX-person quote, but a targeted verbatim search of that fetched article returns no occurrence of 'minimap' or 'tooltips'. The quotes may be genuine but from a different source (a Steam devlog, Bay 12 FotF reply, or another interview) — as cited, they are unsupported. This is exactly the report's stated defect mode: two quoted strings attached to a source that does not contain them. I could not run further web searches (session search budget exhausted); verifying would require searching Bay 12 'Future of the Fortress' replies and DF Steam news posts for 'pixel-perfect minimap'. Remaining mechanism items (tabbed dossier with Thoughts/Personality sub-tabs, cultural vs personal beliefs, four-tab unit list, Work Details with three modes, three-tier announcements, Legends colour-typed hyperlinked categories) were not individually re-verified for the same budget reason and should be treated as unchecked, not endorsed.
correction: Strip the minimap and 'tooltips everywhere' quotes, or re-source them; do not cite them to the Game Developer Steam-release interview.

claim: String connections carry quantified incrimination with three visual encodings: colour = incriminating strength (white→red), width = reliability, animation = direction of flow. Sourced from DevBlog 4 and 10.
reasoning: The SOURCING is stronger than the report credited — I independently fetched both devblogs and both carry the claim near-verbatim. DevBlog 4: 'String colours range from white to red the more incriminating a connection is. The strings also vary in width depending on how reliable a connection is', plus animation showing 'which way the incrimination is flowing'. DevBlog 10 corroborates: incrimination 'comes from sources (so for example in this case the stab wound)', 'facts/connections will allow that incrimination to transfer', and 'you can see the amount of incrimination being transferred by how red the string is, how reliable that connection is by the string's width'. So two independent primary posts agree. BUT the report's own caveat stands and I could not close it: these are pre-release design posts. DevBlog 41 (the 1.0 announcement) discusses kidnapping cases, cover-up missions and an umbrella, and contains NO case-board or evidence-UI content — so it neither confirms nor contradicts survival. I found no shipped-build source describing width-as-reliability. Absence of a removal note is not evidence of survival.
correction: Accurate framing: 'ColePowered's pre-release devblogs (DevBlog 4 and DevBlog 10) specify a three-channel encoding — colour=incrimination magnitude, width=connection reliability, animation=flow direction. Whether all three channels shipped in 1.0 is unverified; treat width-as-reliability as documented design intent with unconfirmed shipped status, not as an observed shipped precedent.' Any design document leaning on this must not cite it as 'proven in a released product'.

claim: 'Unknown Citizen' cards are persistent board objects that resolve in place into a named citizen while RETAINING their existing connections.
reasoning: I could not reach any source that states connections survive identity resolution. Fandom 402, wiki.gg 401, Boilingsteam 404 at the cited URL, Steam guide 429. The report itself concedes this is inferred from community descriptions of a card 'changing'. Under adversarial default this must stay UNVERIFIABLE — and note the inference is not neutral: an engine could equally destroy the unknown-citizen entity and instantiate the named citizen, re-deriving links from the fact graph, which would look identical to a player and produce the same on-screen result while being a completely different mechanism. The report's transferable lesson (stable object identity through progressive disambiguation) depends on which of those it is.
source: No retrievable source; shadows-of-doubt.fandom.com HTTP 402, shadowsofdoubt.wiki.gg HTTP 401
correction: Downgrade to: 'Unidentified people appear as first-class 'unknown' board entities that can be pinned and reasoned over before identification; whether the shipped implementation preserves object identity and edges through resolution, or rebuilds them from the underlying fact graph, is unverified.' To check: in-game observation, or decompiled/modding docs for the citizen-resolution code path.

claim: A citizen profile comprises ~23 identifying attributes, of which only ~6 are actionable.
reasoning: I could not re-verify either half. Fandom (source of the '23') returned 402 as the original researcher reported, and the Steam guide 3017947856 that supposedly grounds the actionable/non-actionable taxonomy returned HTTP 429 on my fetch, so I could not confirm even the part the report calls 'solidly sourced'. I am not disputing the researcher fetched it; I am recording that it is currently single-source, community-authored, and unreproduced by me. Note also a possible category error in their own attribute list: 'pass codes' and 'work hours'/'salary' are not identifying attributes of a person in the same sense as eye colour — if the 23 was assembled by counting rows on a profile UI, it may be mixing identity attributes with case-relevant facts, which would make the ratio itself unstable.
correction: State it as 'a citizen profile carries roughly two dozen recorded attributes, of which only a small minority (~6) are keys to a lookup mechanism; exact count unverified and drawn from a community wiki'. The report is right that the ratio, not the integer, is the argument — but the ratio is community-derived, so the design doc should not cite '23' as a fact.

claim: There is no fingerprint reverse-lookup; the Government Database is name-indexed only.
reasoning: All underlying evidence is player-experience forum material that I could not retrieve (Steam discussion search returned no matching threads on the page I could fetch). More importantly the report's own flagged risk is real and unaddressed: DevBlog 44 (Modifiers Update) and DevBlog 45 postdate the evidence, and neither the researcher nor I audited patch notes for a fingerprint-search addition. A 'no such feature exists' claim is the hardest kind to hold, because it must be true of the CURRENT build, and it is being asserted from undated forum posts.
source: colepowered.com/devblog/ shows DevBlog 44 (Modifiers Update) and DevBlog 45 postdating the cited evidence; underlying Steam threads not retrievable
correction: Say 'players report no fingerprint-indexed search as of the evidence gathered (pre-Sept-2025); not confirmed against current patch notes'. To close it: read DevBlog 44/45 and the Steam patch-note history for database/forensics changes, or observe the terminal in-game.

claim: Nothing is automatically PINNED to the case board and the tutorial misleads players about this; connections auto-form but pinning is manual.
reasoning: This is the weakest item in the report and the report knows it: single outlet, search-summary only, source page 403'd. I could not retrieve it either. The reconciliation with Boilingsteam's 'automatically connected' is explicitly the researcher's own inference, and DevBlog 10 does not settle it — it says only 'You'll also be able to pin them, creating your classic detective corkboard', which describes pinning as a player action but says nothing about whether the game ever pins on the player's behalf, and predates release by years. So the pin/connect distinction is currently an unsourced harmonisation of two sources that appear to disagree.
source: https://colepowered.com/shadows-of-doubt-devblog-10/ (pinning described as player action, pre-release); DayOne review not retrievable (403)
correction: Remove the tutorial-misleads claim, or mark it clearly as one reviewer's impression. The defensible residue: pinning is a deliberate player act rather than an automatic consequence of discovery — and even that needs in-game confirmation before a design doc rests on it.

claim: The all-time most popular Victoria 3 mods are information-density restorations: Complete Pop List (#2), Dense Market Details, Dense Trade Routes Tab, More Spreadsheets (top 12). Source: Wargamer plus Workshop listings.
reasoning: The directional claim survives; the specifics and the cited source do not. All four mods exist and all four appear in the Steam Workshop top 20 by total unique subscribers — real, checkable evidence of demand for density. But (a) the Wargamer mods article cited discusses Necronomicon, an anime mod, Cornwall independence, Belgium deletion and a Prussia recolour — it does not mention any of the four density mods, so that citation is misattributed; (b) the ranks are wrong, not merely drifted; (c) 'the most popular mods ARE density restorations' overstates — the highest-subscribed items are a cheat tool and the Community Mod Framework, with AI mods, translations and performance mods interleaved. Density mods are a strong recurring cluster, not the dominant category.
correction: Accurate version: on the Steam Workshop sorted by total unique subscribers, Dense Market Details ranks ~#3, Complete Pop List ~#12, More Spreadsheets ~#18, and Dense Trade Routes Tab ~#5 but is flagged '[Obsolete for 1.9+]' — i.e. partially absorbed by base-game changes. They sit among top-20 items dominated by cheat/framework/AI mods. The Wargamer citation should be dropped and replaced with the Workshop listing itself, labelled as subscriber-count evidence of player preference, not a developer statement. The recommendation for dense sortable tables alongside designed panels still stands, on weaker but genuine evidence.

claim: Diagnosing a single economic collapse requires cross-referencing at least six unrelated surfaces, with no screen holding the causal chain. Source: a Steam community diagnostic thread.
reasoning: I could not reach the underlying thread. No URL was supplied with the claim, and my web-search budget was exhausted before I could locate it; direct search-engine fetches hit CAPTCHA. The claim is also structurally weak even if the thread exists: 'at least six unrelated surfaces' is an analyst's enumeration imposed on informal community advice, not a count any source states, and a single forum thread is anecdote, not a measured task analysis. This is flagged as the single most-cited claim in the report, so it is the largest unsecured load-bearing point. To verify I would need the actual thread URL, plus ideally two or three independent corroborating sources (e.g. a Reddit diagnostic megathread, a written guide, or a dev acknowledgement of collapse-diagnosis difficulty) to move it from anecdote to pattern.

claim: GPS5 (22 Oct 2024) shipped a 'completely redesigned and revamped' interface as a marquee feature, sits Mostly Negative (~34% of ~388; 213/571 per Steambase), with top reviews attacking the UI.
reasoning: Independently confirmed: Steambase and the Steam store page both give release 22 October 2024, developer/publisher Eversim, 'Mostly Negative'. The store description does carry 'New Interface' as one of three 'WHAT'S NEW IN GPS5' headline sections, worded 'completely redesigned and revamped to be more dynamic and user-friendly', with 4K/screen-size adaptation — so the marquee-feature framing is real, not inferred. Top-rated Steam reviews (all Not Recommended) do attack the interface: Traslo (91.5 hrs) 'The UI is more cramped than ever before. Every last panel could just be 20% bigger', plus 'many new UI panels are worse to use than before' and 'all cramped and hard-coded'. Two problems. First, the quote 'The UI is a disaster' does not appear in the top-rated reviews I retrieved — I could not source it and it should be treated as unverified until located. Second, the report stacks two review figures as if mutually corroborating when they are different populations: '34% of 388' is Steam's default purchaser-only filtered view, while the Steambase count is all reviews. Current live figures: 235 positive / 476 negative of 711 total (~33%), alongside 34% of 388 purchaser reviews. The report's 213/571 is a stale snapshot, not a contradiction. Note also that the top-rated complaints are dominated by bugs, non-functioning economy (GDP won't grow, NaN errors) and annual-release resentment, not UI alone — the UI is one strand of a broader quality complaint. NOTE: this is player-experience evidence (Steam reviews), not a usability study.
correction: Release date, Mostly Negative status, ~33-34% positive, and the store's 'completely redesigned and revamped' New Interface claim are all confirmed, as is the 'more cramped than ever before' review quote. The '213 of 571' figure is a stale sampling (now 235/711) and should be cited with a date; 'The UI is a disaster' is unsourced and should be dropped or re-sourced. The report should also concede that reviewers' dominant complaints are bugs and a broken economy, with UI as one component — the 'restyling without re-architecting' thesis is supported but is not the sole or clearly primary driver of the negative score.

claim: The official 2016 manual states that re-clicking a submitted bill opens a window showing the vote date and estimated votes for EACH PARTY, that the player can always cancel, and that cancellation and refusal each cost popularity.
reasoning: I could not reach the primary source. eversim.com and www.eversim.com both fail TLS (the certificate presented covers only vm353.haisoft.net), that host serves a default Plesk placeholder, web.archive.org is blocked from this environment, and the session's web-search budget was exhausted before I could locate a mirror. No secondary source I could reach quotes the manual. Per the project's own anti-fabrication rule I will not confirm from recollection. This is the report's single most load-bearing recommendation for MERIDIAN, so it must not be carried into a design document on an unverified basis.
source: No accessible source; eversim.com TLS failure (cert altnames: vm353.haisoft.net)
correction: Needs verification against the actual manual text before use. To check: obtain the official Power & Revolution / GPS4 English manual PDF/HTML (via the in-game or Steam-installed manual file, or an Eversim page reached with certificate validation relaxed) and quote the parliament/bill section verbatim, including whether the popularity cost for cancellation versus parliamentary refusal is actually stated or is the researcher's inference.

claim: The manual states budget edits reposition the top indicators into PROVISIONAL twelve-month values to let the player evaluate consequences pre-commit, with saving/expense shown at the bottom and an explicit Confirm.
reasoning: Same blocker as claim 2 — no access to the manual, no reachable secondary source quoting it. I flag one internal risk for the checker who follows: the claim bundles two distinct UI surfaces (the two indicators at the top of every ministry screen, and the saving/expense readout plus Confirm inside the Budget tab) into a single 'edit/preview/commit separation'. Even if both sentences exist in the manual, whether they compose into one coherent preview-then-commit flow in the running product is a design inference, not a documented property, and the report should label it as such. The product's own defining defect per this project's framing is documentation asserting properties that do not exist — a manual sentence is evidence about the manual, not about the build.
source: No accessible source; eversim.com TLS failure
correction: Verify each half separately against the manual, and separately verify in-game that the provisional indicators actually update live before Confirm. Downgrade 'a real edit/preview/commit separation exists in this product' to 'the manual describes components that would constitute one' unless observed in play.

claim: Charisma, network of influence and level of protection are hidden and unmaskable via secret-service investigations; the character sheet carries glyphs encoding the player's epistemic state (tape recorder = investigation running, file = you hold secret-service info).
reasoning: Same access blocker. I can neither confirm the quoted phrase 'can be unmasked by launching investigations through your secret service' nor the glyph inventory (assembly icon, medal, hospital bed, gate, heart, ballot box, tape recorder, file). The glyph list is long, specific and internally plausible, which is exactly the shape of both a faithful transcription and a confabulation — plausibility is not evidence. The load-bearing inference ('a partial intelligence-product model already exists') depends on the tape-recorder and file glyphs specifically, i.e. on the two items that encode the PLAYER's knowledge state rather than world state; those two are the ones to verify first, since the other six encode world facts and would not support the argument.
source: No accessible source; eversim.com TLS failure
correction: Verify the two epistemic glyphs (tape recorder, file) against the manual's character-sheet legend or a screenshot of a character sheet before building any MERIDIAN argument on an existing intelligence-product model.

claim: God'n Spy is a separately sold add-on exposing hidden engine variables via buttons on ~28 named screens, and its manual states that modifying a variable disqualifies the player's score from the global online ranking.
reasoning: I could not confirm even the existence of the product from an accessible source: the Eversim site is unreachable, web.archive.org is blocked, DuckDuckGo returned a CAPTCHA on the God'n Spy query, and I could not resolve the Steam app ID for the Power & Revolution base game to enumerate its DLC. Two things about the claim's construction warrant scepticism independent of sourcing. First, 'roughly twenty-eight named screens' is hedged with 'roughly' while the accompanying list is enumerated exactly — a count should either be exact from a source or absent. Second, the ranking-disqualification rule is separately asserted in the mechanism list for the world-generation sliders ('to record a GAME score the player must be in Realistic mode with all four world sliders centred'); one plausible failure mode is that a single general anti-cheat ranking rule has been attributed twice, once to the God'n Spy manual and once to the base manual. That does not make either wrong, but the two should be sourced independently rather than treated as mutually reinforcing.
source: No accessible source; eversim.com TLS failure, web.archive.org blocked, DuckDuckGo CAPTCHA
correction: Confirm God'n Spy's existence and paid-DLC status from its Steam DLC page (retrievable once the base game's app ID is resolved), then quote the ranking-disqualification sentence from the God'n Spy manual specifically, separately from the sliders/Realistic-mode ranking rule. Give the screen count exactly or drop it.

claim: Attribute masking renders unscouted attributes as RANGES in the same cell position as exact values (e.g. 'Passing 8-16'), rather than hiding the row
reasoning: Multiple independent sources corroborate that partial scouting knowledge yields a RANGE of numbers rather than a hidden field — Passion4FM and community guidance surfaced via search state 'if there is a range between numbers they need scouting'. So the core 'render ignorance in place' behaviour survives. What I could NOT verify from any fetched source is the specific rendering detail the report asserts as fact: that the range occupies the identical cell in the identical three-column Technical/Mental/Physical grid, that the example '8-16' is real rather than illustrative, or the claim that there is 'no separate unknown players screen'. Also unverified-and-contested: a Sports Interactive community thread ('How can I disable attribute masking in FM 26') indicates the masking toggle that existed in Advanced Options may not be exposed in FM26, so 'on by default' is version-specific and should not be stated flatly for the whole series.
source: community.sports-interactive.com/forums/topic/594042-how-can-i-disable-attribute-masking-in-fm-26/ ; reddit.com/r/footballmanagergames/comments/voaqjs/ ; Passion4FM (snippet only, full page not fetchable)
correction: Say: partially-scouted attributes degrade to a numeric RANGE in place rather than disappearing (confirmed in principle); treat the exact cell layout, the '8-16' example, and 'default on in all versions' as unverified. Masking applies only to players NOT at your club who are not fully scouted (Reddit, player-experience evidence).

claim: The star bar encodes uncertainty as a distinct colour channel: BLACK = assessor's uncertainty portion, WHITE = unresolved future range, alongside gold/silver for value
reasoning: The value channel is corroborated: gold = first-team scale, silver/grey = below first-team scale, and the specific 'five silver is less than half a gold' figure appears in community sources. The uncertainty channel is corroborated in EXISTENCE but not in the two-distinct-colours form the report needs. The aggregated source reading I obtained says white stars 'previously black in earlier versions' represent uncertainty about current or potential ability — i.e. black and white are the SAME channel across different releases, not two simultaneous semantic channels. That directly contradicts the report's four-way gold/silver/black/white simultaneous scheme, and it also explains the conflicting Steam-thread player who calls black stars 'estimated potential'. This is the report's strongest recommendation and it currently rests on an unresolved conflict, not on a verified reading.
source: Aggregated community explanations of FM star colours retrieved via DuckDuckGo HTML results (specialist FM guide pages; full pages 403/unfetchable). No primary SI documentation obtained.
correction: Defensible version: FM star bars carry TWO channels — a value channel (gold vs silver denoting which scale the rating sits on) and an uncertainty channel rendered as a differently-coloured extension of the bar (black in older releases, white in newer ones) showing the span the assessor has not resolved. Do NOT assert black and white as two distinct co-existing meanings without a version-specific primary source or a screenshot.

claim: Ground truth (CA/PA, 1-200) is never displayed to the player on any screen, in any view mode, at any knowledge level; reading it requires an external editor or database tool
reasoning: The 0/1-200 scale and its absence from the normal UI are corroborated. But the absolute framing is wrong in a way that matters for your argument. CA/PA are exposed inside the product by the In-Game Editor, which is an official first-party paid SI DLC, not an 'external editor or database tool'. Community skins/mods also surface CA/PA as plain numbers in ordinary profile screens. So the boundary SI actually enforces is 'not in the default UI, revealed only by an explicitly-purchased god-mode layer', not 'never rendered anywhere'. There is also an unreliable user report of CA/PA being visible on an FM23 Steam build versus hidden on FM24 Epic — I would not repeat this; it is almost certainly the reporter having the editor DLC on one platform, but I could not confirm either way.
source: Aggregated results incl. FM community discussion of In-Game Editor CA/PA fields and CA/PA-display mods; scale figure (e.g. 198 CA / 200 PA) from community sources. Player-experience evidence, not SI documentation.
correction: State: CA/PA drive the simulation and are absent from every default-UI screen at every knowledge level; they are reachable only via the official In-Game Editor DLC or third-party skins/database tools. The MERIDIAN analogy still holds, but as 'ground truth is behind an opt-in debug/authoring layer', not 'ground truth is unrenderable'.

claim: Mechanism-list scan: touchline tablet (8 panels, framed as 'sent by your analysts'), scout grade 'A to E, A+ strongest possible', and the seasonal subscription-review prompt
reasoning: Three assertions in the mechanism list look inflated or conflated and should not be built on. (a) TOUCHLINE TABLET: the official FM26 features page describes Match Momentum and Pass Maps as 'data visualisations incorporated into the Touchline Tablet display' — automated visualisations, with no diegetic 'sent by your analysts' framing anywhere I could find. The panel roster I could enumerate is six (Match Momentum, Shot Map home/away, Action Zones, Average Positions home/away), not eight, and the '8 panels / resizable / collapsible to one column / competition-icon top-left / tablet-icon bottom-right' detail is unverified and is pure trade dress. (b) SCOUT GRADE SCALE: a community example describes a scout grade of A+ combined with a data-analyst grade of F — an F grade means the scale is not bounded at E, so 'A to E' is likely wrong, and the report also conflates the SCOUT grade with the separate ANALYST grade that is blended into the overall recommendation. (c) SUBSCRIPTION REVIEW: I found no evidence at all for the Personal Assistant proactively prompting a subscription review at the start of every season — treat as unverified. (d) 'One FM26 pack advertises around 40 views' is an unsourced marketing number; drop it, it carries no design weight.
source: https://www.footballmanager.com/features/gameplay-upgrades ; fmdossier.dev/guides/reading-a-scout-report ; community discussion of scout vs analyst grades
correction: Rewrite the tablet mechanism as: an in-match secondary surface hosting selectable analytical visualisations (momentum over time, pass maps, shot maps, action zones, average positions) so the time-pressured view is user-composed — drop the analyst-diegesis, the panel count and all chrome placement. Rewrite the grade mechanism as: recommendation is a letter grade produced by BLENDING a scout assessment with a separate data-analyst assessment, which can disagree sharply — that disagreement is the transferable idea, not the alphabet range. Delete the seasonal subscription prompt and the 40-views figure unless sourced.

claim: Changelog contains verbatim evidence that unknown values get a reserved rendering rather than being blanked/defaulted (Rdn+Supply white when unknown; black hexes show 'Unknown' in Zone Tab), and that FOW leaks were repeatedly found in reports rather than on the map (Battle Reports, MTH Overview Report).
reasoning: All four quotes are verbatim-accurate in the PDF: 'Fixed color of Rdn + Supply in Unit Tab to white if the variable is actually unknown due to lack of recon.' (line 862); 'Black hexes will now show "Unknown" in Zone Tab (Elver)' (line 2722); 'Made many improvements to the details of Battle Reports. They now properly use the FOW and hide what should be hidden.' (line 976); 'Fixed FOW with the MTH Overview Report ( was showing MTHs you should not know yet)' (line 2482). The report-not-map FOW pattern is in fact STRONGER than claimed — adjacent lines show 'Fixed FOW with the individual MTH Reports', 'Hiding shareholders from MTH Overview if you do not have the recon on those Majors yet', and an MTH Overview recon-rules fix. The overreach is interpretive: the changelog documents that unknown values are rendered distinctly and labelled 'Unknown', but nowhere states that white is a colour RESERVED for unknown-ness. 'Reserved treatment' is design inference presented as documented fact.
correction: Evidence supports: unknown values receive a distinct visual state and an explicit 'Unknown' label rather than being blanked or silently defaulted. It does NOT establish that any specific colour is reserved exclusively for unknown-ness. The MERIDIAN 'unknown as present row' and 'projection chokepoint' recommendations survive on the weaker, accurate framing.

claim: Mechanism list: logistics has 'three named diagnosis tools' — a Bottlenecks highlighter, a Logistics Preview, and a Traffic Signs mode.
reasoning: Two of the three verify against the changelog as named features: 'Logistics Preview Mode' appears repeatedly (lines 2681, 2692, 3188, 3199) and traffic signs appear as a real per-hex mechanic with truck/rail rules (lines 2636, 3895, 4744). The 'Bottlenecks highlighter' does not. Every occurrence of 'bottleneck' in the 5,369-line changelog refers to GRAPHICS ENGINE performance — 'attack rendering bottlenecks in other parts of the game in the future' (2673, 3178) — not a logistics diagnosis tool. This is not proof of fabrication; the feature may be documented in the 400-page manual or visible in-game, and WebSearch was unavailable to check community guides. But it is presented inside a list whose neighbours are changelog-sourced, and that asymmetry is invisible to a reader. To verify: check the Shadow Empire manual's logistics chapter or the in-game right-bar overlay tab for a control named 'Bottlenecks'. The same caveat applies to the report's Steam-community-guide citation about the Cabinet screen having 'a better visualization' than MNG Leaders, which I could not check without search.
source: https://ftp.matrixgames.com/pub/ShadowEmpire/whatsnew.pdf (lines 2673, 3178 for rendering bottlenecks; 2681/2692/3188/3199 for Logistics Preview Mode; 2636/3895/4744 for traffic signs)
correction: Downgrade to two changelog-verified logistics diagnosis tools (Logistics Preview Mode, Traffic Signs) plus a third, 'Bottlenecks', that is asserted without a located source and must be marked unverified until confirmed against the manual or a screenshot.

claim: FP2 Council is exactly 100 Delegates apportioned by population share; 51 votes standard, 67 (two-thirds) for Rule laws; For (blue) / Against (red) / Hesitant (faint blend); Hesitant have an even chance either way.
reasoning: The numeric and behavioural half is transcribed accurately from the cited source. The Council page states verbatim: 'The Council is made up of 100 Delegates, with each Faction and Community having a number of Delegates equal to the percentage of the total Population who are members of that Faction or Community'; standard laws need at least 51 votes; 'Laws in the Rule category, which increase the personal power of the Steward, require a supermajority of 67 votes'; and '"Hesitant" Delegates have an even chance of voting "for" or "against" the proposal.' HOWEVER the COLOUR assignments are NOT on that page. I re-fetched it and it names the three states but does not specify blue/red/faint-blend anywhere. That detail is presented as wiki-sourced but is not. Separately, everything here rests on one community wiki; I could not reach a first-party corroborant (Fandom returns HTTP 402, and I had no search budget to locate official patch notes).
correction: Keep the 100/51/67 and even-chance-Hesitant figures but attribute the colour coding correctly — it is unsourced, most likely the author's own play memory, and must be labelled as such (the report labels play knowledge elsewhere, so this is an inconsistency, not a habit). Also downgrade all four numbers from 'wiki-confirmed' to 'single-community-wiki, uncorroborated'.

claim: Negotiation offers exactly three RANDOMLY-selected terms of which the player picks one to Promise; one Promise per group, three overall; failure lowers that group's relations and overall Trust.
reasoning: The wording is quoted correctly: 'The game will randomly pick 3 terms the group wants, and you have to choose 1 of those 3 to Promise', with one active promise per group, maximum three total, and breaking one decreasing relations and trust. But note the source's own phrasing is 'terms the group WANTS' — i.e. the pool is motivation-derived and the RANDOMNESS is only in which three of that group's wants are surfaced. That is materially different from 'randomly-selected terms'. Since you flagged this as the claim most load-bearing for your contrast recommendation, that distinction is exactly the one that damages it: the design is already a random SAMPLE of a motivated pool, not random content.
correction: 'The game randomly draws 3 terms from the set of terms that group wants, and the player must Promise one of the 3.' Your recommendation to MERIDIAN should contrast against random SAMPLING of motivated demands, not against random demands — otherwise you are arguing with a straw version of the mechanic.

claim: Trust is 0–100 with five named tiers Revered / Respected / Accepted / Tolerated / Despised; Despised triggers an ultimatum quest to recover to 25% or lose.
reasoning: Structure confirmed against the cited page: 0–100, can go negative, five tiers, ultimatum at Despised to earn trust back from 0% to 25%, failure is game over, no direct bonus at high Trust but it is spendable (e.g. Rush Researcher). One discrepancy: the fetched page renders the top tier as 'Revived', not 'Revered'. One of the two is a transcription error and I cannot tell which without the game or a second source. 'Revered' is the semantically plausible reading in a trust ladder, so the wiki is the likelier typo — but the report should not silently normalise a source it is quoting.
correction: Cite the tier list as 'Revered (rendered "Revived" on the source page — likely a wiki typo, unverified) / Respected / Accepted / Tolerated / Despised'. The structural recommendation for macro saturation survives regardless; only the label needs the caveat.

claim: FP1 enforces a law-signing cooldown of roughly 24 in-game hours, tracked by a white radial around the Book of Laws HUD icon.
reasoning: I could not reach any source for this. The TheGamer guide URL pattern I tried returned 404 and I had no search budget to locate the actual article; frostpunk.fandom.com returns HTTP 402 to WebFetch. So I can neither confirm the existence of the radial nor the 24-hour figure. Your own instinct is right and I'd go further: the report describes this as 'well corroborated' but presents exactly one guide-site citation for it, which is not corroboration. A single tertiary games-guide page is the weakest evidence class in this report and it is being used to assert a specific quantitative duration.

source: No reachable source; https://www.thegamer.com/frostpunk-laws-guide/ returned 404, Fandom returned 402

claim: Multiple INDEPENDENT reviewers criticised FP2's UI (overlay tooltips, shifting panels, delayed feedback) and independently reported that the citizens-to-factions shift reduced the moral weight of decisions.
reasoning: I could not retrieve any of the four cited reviews. The Digital Trends and ScreenRant URLs I constructed both 404'd, and without search I cannot recover the real URLs. I therefore cannot check whether the direct quotes are accurate, whether all four outlets actually make BOTH claims, or whether the four are genuinely independent (games-press reviews frequently converge on a shared critical framing after a review-embargo cycle, which is weak evidence of independent observation). This is the empirical backbone of your strongest recommendation and it is currently unaudited.
