If the UI-criticism claim and the moral-weight claim do not each have multiple genuinely separate outlets behind them, state them at the strength the evidence actually carries.
source: Cited review URLs not reachable; no search budget to locate originals

claim: Mechanism-list scan for fabricated / conflated / non-existent features.
reasoning: Most entries check out against the wiki. Verified: active law changeable by Council vote at any time if another is available; synergy and contradiction labels on law cards; in-session support/opposition indicators; the Tension orb filling with black or red liquid, roads recolouring, and at Catastrophic 'all the pathways will turn red, UI will be covered in black liquid' before exile; the 10-week recess; Guided Voting and the 12-week Emergency Session cooldown. THREE problems. (a) The law-card magnitudes are misquoted: the report cites '+12 points in [Ideology]' and '−0.002 [Food]/Day per capita'; the page shows '+7 points in Adaptation' and '+0.002 Food/Day per capita' — the number and the SIGN are both wrong, so these read as reconstructed-from-memory figures presented as quotes. (b) The Factions/Communities entry overreaches: the Factions page supports one-vs-three ideologies and the negotiability contrast, but I found no support for 'generate many Hesitant delegates' or for the 'except on Rule-category laws' exception; both are presented in quotation-flavoured prose. (c) The Command Radial entry (three spokes, left trigger, pause-and-blur, context-sensitive right trigger, Extended City View on X) is entirely uncorroborated — I reached no console-edition source at all. Given this report's stated defect mode is documentation asserting properties that don't exist, an unverified console-UI section is the highest-risk item in the list.
correction: Fix the law-card magnitudes to the source's actual values and signs, or drop the specific numbers. Strip or explicitly de-source the 'many Hesitant delegates' and 'Rule-law exception' assertions about Factions. Quarantine the entire Command Radial entry as unverified pending a first-party source (11 bit console dev-blog or the console UI itself).

claim: Old World shows mechanical consequences of each event option BEFORE commitment, by design; Johnson contrasts King of Dragon Pass ('you are meant to just intuit the results') with Old World ('the direct result of each of your decisions needs to be clear'). Part 3.
reasoning: Both quotes are verbatim and in context. Actual text: 'Dragon Pass doesn't tell you the effects of your decisions, you are meant to just intuit the results, which works for some games but not for Old World, a game where transparency is one of the most important design aesthetics. The direct result of each of your decisions needs to be clear.' The DESIGN INTENT claim is fully confirmed. Two corrections: (a) the series is on designer-notes.com (Johnson's personal blog, a GDC-postmortem write-up), NOT mohawkgames.com — the report's citation locator is wrong and a reader will 404; (b) the accompanying mechanism entry asserts effects are printed 'on the option itself, visible before the player clicks', and a reviewer of the 2021 build states the opposite: 'You have to hover over each option to see what the effect will be: I have no idea why this information is not simply included on the relevant button instead.' The information is pre-commitment but was gated behind hover, not attached to the button.
correction: Cite https://www.designer-notes.com/my-elephant-in-the-room-part-3/. State: consequences are available before commitment (design intent explicit and quotable), but at least in the 2021 build they were revealed by hovering each option rather than rendered on the option itself. Do not claim 'attached to the option' without a current screenshot.

claim: Nested/pinnable 'Infinite tooltips' where keywords spawn further tooltips that stay on screen simultaneously; Johnson credits Jon Shafer. Shift/Shift+click modifier rests only on secondary write-ups.
reasoning: Confirmed from Part 1: Johnson credits Shafer, who 'pioneered infinite tooltips', and says 'you can go as far as you want. We have Infinite tooltips!' Steam's own store copy independently confirms 'nested tooltips'. However Part 1 describes only opening 'a new tooltip off of the previous one' — it does NOT establish PINNING or simultaneous persistence. That behaviour is corroborated only by player/critic experience: the Ancient World Magazine reviewer describes 'Endless amounts of tooltips that you can freeze in place in order to hover your mouse over some tiny text that will pop up another tooltip' and 'At some points, most of my screen was filled with tooltips!' The specific keys (Shift to freeze, Shift+click to lock) I could NOT verify against any controls list, manual or patch note — treat as unverified.
correction: Nesting + Shafer attribution: primary-sourced, safe. Freeze-in-place and simultaneous on-screen stacking: label as critic-reported player experience. The Shift / Shift+click bindings must be marked UNVERIFIED until checked against the in-game controls screen.

claim: No in-game view of events involving a given character, per dev quote in Steam thread 3815166629925906635.
reasoning: The quote is verbatim and the speaker (Fluffster) is a developer: 'There isn't anywhere in game that shows all the events that have occurred involving a character, though it sounds like a good suggestion for a future addition.' But the SAME thread contains the dev's follow-up: 'The next version due out this Wednesday is going to have a list of events that a character has been involved in shown on their tooltip, thanks to your suggestion.' The report's framing as a gap lasting 'for years' is not supported by the thread, and presenting it as a live gap would be a misrepresentation of a source the report itself cites.
correction: Reframe: the gap existed and was acknowledged by a developer, then was CLOSED within roughly one patch cycle — a character event-history list was added to the character tooltip. The transferable lesson is the fix and its turnaround, not a persistent absence.

claim: Ancient World Magazine's UI criticism is genuine and verbatim; Interactive Pasts contradicts it ('It doesn't take up too much of the screen and is intuitive'), and any citation must include the disagreement.
reasoning: Both AWM quotes are verbatim and in context: 'The interface is wretched: a mess of small fonts and tiny icons' and 'Why is the portrait of my leader so large in the bottom left corner if all of his stats are hidden inside a tooltip? You cannot click on it, only hover to get a tooltip.' The review is in fact HARSHER than the report conveys — it also calls it 'the worst interface in a game that I have seen in a long time' and faults the reliance on tooltips and the lack of visual hierarchy. On the other side: Interactive Pasts does praise the UI ('pretty sleek', unobtrusive, quick to acclimatise), so the disagreement is real — but I could not confirm the specific sentence 'It doesn't take up too much of the screen and is intuitive' verbatim from the fetched article. Do not present it in quotation marks.
correction: Keep the AWM quotes (verbatim, and note it is a 2021-build review titled 'A deeply flawed historical strategy game'). Keep the disagreement, but paraphrase Interactive Pasts as praising the UI as sleek and unobtrusive rather than quoting the sentence, unless the exact wording is re-confirmed.

claim: Mechanism-list scan: items that look fabricated, conflated, or unverified.
reasoning: Verified: nested tooltips and undo are both in Steam's own copy ('the ability to undo mistaken commands and nested tooltips'); role-contextual trait effects, cause-of-death field and opinion graphs are all in Update #132 verbatim. NOT verified by any source I reached, and each should be marked UNVERIFIED rather than asserted: (1) the 'Roleplay' setting that suppresses pre-commitment effect display — no patch note, wiki page or store text found; this is the single most fabrication-prone entry because it is exactly the kind of plausible inverse-feature a model invents; (2) undo extending to narrative EVENT decisions — Steam says 'undo mistaken commands', and the mechanism entry hedges this to 'per reviewers' without naming one; (3) F1 as universal context-sensitive encyclopedia link, including the quoted fragments 'consistently... regardless of context' and 'F1 to open encyclopedia entry'; (4) the advanced-help-text verbosity setting; (5) the numeric specifics — Legitimacy converting to Orders at 0.1/point/turn, ~3 moves via fatigue, shared traits +20/+40, ambition tier bands 'after 2 and before 5', ten ambitions to win, '3,000+' events; (6) the family-tree quote 'Family tree UI amendments to highlight leader and heirs' and 'Portrait Interpolation'. None of these are refuted — most are individually plausible — but none were confirmed, and the report presents several inside quotation marks as if from patch notes.
correction: Every quoted patch-note fragment in the mechanism list needs a specific update number and URL, as Update #132 has. Until then, downgrade the 'Roleplay' setting, event-decision undo, the F1 behaviour, the advanced-help-text setting, and all numeric constants to UNVERIFIED. Verify by fetching the specific update posts under https://mohawkgames.com/blog/ (paginated, ~monthly) and searching them for the exact strings.

claim: Obra Dinn validates only in complete sets of three (identity + fate + responsible party), last six in sets of two; corroborated by two independent design analyses.
reasoning: The core batch mechanic IS directly verifiable. Wikipedia states verbatim: 'correct fates are validated only in sets of three, with the exception of the last six fates discovered in a playthrough, which are validated in sets of two until closer to the end.' So 'never confirm a single probe' survives — this is the one claim in the report with a solid spine. But three sub-assertions do not survive intact. (a) The report drops the qualifier 'until closer to the end', and omits what Wikipedia adds next: once 58 of 60 souls are correctly logged, the final two are validated automatically — i.e. the batch rule DOES break down at the endgame, which matters if MERIDIAN copies the rule as an invariant. (b) Wikipedia describes the validated content as 'names and causes of death'; the specific tripartite 'identity + fate + responsible party all correct simultaneously' formulation is NOT sourced by the citation given and I could not verify it (obradinn.fandom.com returned HTTP 402). (c) I could not reach the 'two independent design analyses', so the corroboration is asserted, not shown.
correction: Accurate version: fates are validated in sets of three; the last six are validated in sets of two 'until closer to the end'; and once 58 of 60 are correct the remaining two validate automatically. Treat the exact slot composition (whether the responsible party must be simultaneously correct) as unverified pending a primary source. Drop the 'corroborated by two independent analyses' phrasing unless those analyses are cited by URL.

claim: Papers, Please inspect mode lets the player highlight ANY TWO addressable items including an empty counter slot and a rulebook line; Pope kept it a separate mode so the player could 'express their intent clearly'.
reasoning: The report already concedes dukope.com returned 403 and that the quote comes from a third-party scrape. I attempted the TIGSource devlog thread directly (forums.tigsource.com) and it also returned HTTP 403. So the quote is now two removes from any source anyone in this chain has actually read. This is precisely the failure mode the project is trying to avoid: a load-bearing design recommendation resting on an unread quote. Separately, the 'ANY TWO addressable pieces of information' framing is stated with a confidence the sourcing cannot support — in particular 'an EMPTY counter slot' as a selectable referent is an unusual affordance that I could verify from no source at all, and it may be an inference from the game's missing-document rejections rather than an observed interaction.
source: forums.tigsource.com/index.php?topic=40832.0 — HTTP 403; dukope.com — HTTP 403 per original report
correction: Do not cite the 'express their intent clearly' quote until someone reads it at dukope.com or the TIGSource thread (both need a browser session, not WebFetch). The safest defensible version, verifiable by direct play rather than by quote: inspect mode requires the player to select two referents and the game reports only whether they conflict. Verify the empty-slot case specifically before building an interaction on it.

claim: Golden Idol's shipped feedback indicator communicates that TWO OR FEWER slots are incorrect, and locks segments that are entirely correct.
reasoning: I could not reach either cited source. The Game Developer deep-dive URL I constructed resolved to the publication's homepage (no such article at that path), and the Adventure Game Hotspot review URL served a different page entirely (a trailer announcement). So neither the exact numeric threshold nor the segment-lock behaviour is currently backed by anything I read. Note the report's own hedging — it calls the AGH corroboration 'weaker form' ('wildly incorrect, or only a couple words off'), which is a qualitative binary and does NOT establish a threshold of exactly two. A designer interview describing an early or considered design is also easy to mistake for shipped behaviour; the report asserts 'shipped indicator' without showing the source says shipped.
source: gamedeveloper.com (constructed URL → homepage, article not located); adventuregamehotspot.com/review/1132 (served unrelated page)
correction: Retain the directional principle — feedback is distance-like and non-diagnostic (it never says WHICH slots) — but strip the specific '(two or fewer)' number until the Game Developer interview is located by title and read. The segment-lock-on-fully-correct behaviour should be confirmed by direct play; it is cheap to check and it is the half of the claim MERIDIAN's design actually depends on.

claim: In Orwell, committing one of several conflicting datachunks permanently deactivates its counterparts, AND the game warns the player when they have not yet found all conflicting versions.
reasoning: The cited Osmotic Studios deep dive could not be retrieved (constructed Game Developer URL resolved to the homepage). This claim is a compound of two mechanics with very different plausibility, and the report presents them at equal confidence. The irreversibility half is widely consistent with how Orwell is generally described. The second half — a proactive warning that unfound conflicting versions exist — is the more surprising assertion, because it would partly defeat the irreversible-commitment tension the first half creates, and it is also the half MERIDIAN's 'incomplete-evidence commit gate' is directly modelled on. A game that warns 'there may be more versions' in the abstract is a very different design from one that knows and says you have not found them all; those two readings are easy to conflate from a single sentence in an interview.
source: gamedeveloper.com Orwell deep dive — URL not located, fetch returned publication homepage
correction: Before this becomes MERIDIAN's model, establish which of two readings the source supports: (i) a generic irreversibility warning shown on every conflicted commit, versus (ii) a state-aware warning conditioned on undiscovered counterparts existing. Only (ii) justifies an incomplete-evidence commit gate. Locate the deep dive by article title, or verify in-game.

claim: UNVERIFIED AND FLAGGED: Her Story's search returns only the first five matching clips.
reasoning: I independently checked the researcher's own flag and it holds up — Wikipedia describes searching transcriptions, user tags, and the red/green database checker, and states nothing about a result cap of five or any other number. I have the same untested prior the researcher does, which is exactly why neither of us should assert it: two models sharing a prior is not corroboration. Credit where due — flagging this rather than dressing it up is the correct behaviour and it is the most trustworthy item in the report.
correction: Keep it out of the design document until verified. Cheapest check is direct play — run a deliberately common query (e.g. a stopword-adjacent term) and count returned clips. Sam Barlow has discussed the search design in interviews, but I could not reach one, so play-verification is the reliable route.

claim: No-fire refusal messages are a documented closed enumeration of ~30 named reasons, each with live numeric values substituted and a stated 'Solution:', including one naming the governing doctrine setting and its risk (manual §9.2.8)
reasoning: §9.2.8 'MY @#%^@#% WEAPON WON'T FIRE!!!' exists and contains 32 'Solution:' lines against ~32 named reasons, with bracketed live-value templates exactly as quoted ('Target speed [target speed in knots] is much higher than the weapon's maximum target speed ([weapon maximum target speed in knots])'; 'Weapon cannot engage this target for another [time value in sec] (OODA loop limitation)'). The ambiguity reason and its risk-bearing solution are verbatim correct, including 'set the firing unit's "Shooting ambiguous targets" doctrine setting to "Optimistic" or even "Ignore"... Beware that this means a large probability that the weapon will miss altogether.' The defect is 'closed enumeration': the manual explicitly frames it as 'Here is a summary of the no-fire explanations', not an exhaustive list, and the engine-side check is described only as 'an exhaustive checklist of conditions'. Also worth knowing before you copy the template: the manual contains a copy-paste error — the cross-range reason is printed with the downrange wording ('The target's downrange ambiguity...') while its prose describes 'target cross-range instead of distance ambiguity'. And the '[some percentage]' placeholder is left unresolved in the manual text.
correction: ~32 documented reasons, each with a 'Solution:' line, presented by the manual as a SUMMARY of no-fire explanations rather than a closed/exhaustive enumeration. The doctrine-naming ambiguity reason is real and quoted correctly, but appears twice with a printing error (cross-range case mislabelled 'downrange').

claim: Mechanism-list scan: Time Compression drop-down is '1x / 2x / 5x / turbo-flame'; Message Log options table has 'four columns'; the 'New Contact' message type was 'split into' five per-domain types
reasoning: Three inaccuracies found in the mechanism list, all minor but all the kind that erode trust in a citation-heavy document. (1) Time compression: the manual lists 'The options are 1x (real time), 2x time acceleration (twice as fast), 5x, 15x, and the flame/turbo option... There is one option beyond flame called double flame or coarse fidelity.' The list omits 15x and double-flame/coarse-fidelity entirely. (2) Message Log routing table: the manual says 'The Message Log has four columns' and then lists FIVE bullets — Message Type, Show on Message Log, Raise Pop-Up, Show Balloon, and 'Timescale X1: Immediately trigger the game to return from whatever time acceleration it was in to 1x.' The report reproduced the manual's 'four' and dropped the fifth control, which is the most interesting one for MERIDIAN (an automatic speed-clamp on event class). (3) The UI addendum says the New Contact type is SUPPLEMENTED, not split: 'The existing "New Contact" message type is supplemented by more focused "New Air Contact", "New Surface Contact", "New Submerged Contact" and "New Land Contact" types', with New Mine Contact added separately in v1.11. Everything else I spot-checked in the list is verbatim accurate: 'Skunk #_' / Admiral Kuznetsov ORBAT example, the access-gating quote, Drop Contact 'probably just a whale anyway', Contact Report / Emissions / Spotted Hosted Units, message-log grouping and unread highlighting, the five Awareness values, Can Auto-Track Civilians, Collective Responsibility, the 'inherited' doctrine text, override prompts, Player Editable checkboxes, four doctrine tabs, and CTRL+Shift+F9.
correction: Time compression is 1x / 2x / 5x / 15x / flame-turbo / double-flame (coarse fidelity). The Message Log options table is labelled 'four columns' but documents five controls, the fifth being 'Timescale X1' — an automatic drop to real time on a given message type. 'New Contact' was supplemented by per-domain types, not replaced by them.

claim: DE's check UI itemises individual modifiers with source names and signed values inside the check panel
reasoning: Both the wiki.gg Skills and Checks pages confirm only that white/red checks 'will almost always have a list of positive and/or negative modifiers, sometimes up to ten different ones' arising from dialogue choices, clothing, thoughts and drugs. Neither page describes the on-screen presentation. I could not obtain a screenshot or a primary source (dev talk, UI teardown) showing per-row source names with signed values; the web-search budget for this session was exhausted, so absence of evidence here is partly a tooling limit, not proof of absence. The researcher's own labelling (personal play recall) is honest and should stay labelled.
correction: Downgrade to: the game tracks and displays modifiers plus a probability; whether each modifier is rendered as a named, signed row is UNVERIFIED. Verify with a captured screenshot of a white check panel before any design doc treats 'itemised provenance of a probability' as shipped precedent.

claim: Difficulty tier → target mapping Easy 8–9 … Impossible 17+, success = skill + modifiers + 2d6 ≥ target
reasoning: wiki.gg Skills confirms the formula verbatim ('skill level + modifiers + dice roll, where an equal or higher result wins') and every tier value through Godly 16. The one deviation: the table lists Impossible as '17-18, 20', i.e. a set of observed values with gaps, not an open-ended '17+'.
correction: Impossible is documented as 17–18 and 20 (no 19 instance recorded), not 'Impossible 17+'. The 'plain label locked to a hard number' inference survives intact — arguably strengthened, since even the top tier is enumerated rather than open-ended.

claim: Natural 2 always fails and natural 12 always succeeds, so no displayed probability is ever 0% or 100%
reasoning: wiki.gg confirms the base rule but with an explicit exception the report omits: 'Rolling two ones always loses (critical failure), and two sixes always wins (critical success), unless changed by The Precarious World.' That Thought shifts the thresholds to 2–3 auto-fail and 11–12 auto-succeed, moving extreme outcomes from ~2.7% to ~8.3%. Critically, the wiki states this change 'is not reflected in the game's UI'.
correction: The floor/ceiling is a DEFAULT, mutable by an internalised Thought — and when mutated, the displayed percentage becomes materially wrong and the UI never says so. For a project whose defining defect is documentation asserting properties that do not hold, this is the more instructive finding than the clamping rule itself: the shipped precedent includes a state where the number on screen is a lie. Any MERIDIAN recommendation should require that modifiers to the probability model propagate into the displayed probability, not just clamp the endpoints.

claim: HOI4 intel: army 5% = fielded division count ±80% 'narrows with more intel'; 40% = equipment stockpiles ±50%; 50% = exact division counts by template; civilian 10% = factory counts ±50%; civilian 40% = exact counts. Estimate is ALWAYS shown, never hidden.
reasoning: I pulled the full four threshold tables from the HOI4 wiki Intel page. The GENERAL MECHANISM is real and well-documented: intel is a 0-100% percentage per category per country pair, and revealed values genuinely carry explicit ± error bands with the literal phrase 'narrows with more intel'. So the load-bearing design point — a shipped mainstream strategy game renders quantified uncertainty — survives. But roughly half the specific thresholds in the report are wrong, and the navy ladder appears largely invented or misremembered. The report also asserts a property the tables contradict.
correction: Correct per wiki: ARMY — 5% total fielded count (±80%) [correct]; 10% fielded manpower (±50%); 20% basic template info (report said 30%); 30% equipment stockpiles (±50%) (report said 40%); 40% template + division count (±100%) (report said 50% = EXACT counts by template — wrong, and it omits the ±100% band, which is the single most striking data point in the whole system); 70% exact fielded count / template + exact division count; 80% exact template/division, variant stats, immune to Plant False Intelligence; 90% immune AND reveals existing fake intel; 100% exact special-forces count. Report's '0% = total divisions unknown (±70% fuzziness)' is a conflation: at 0% total divisions are '???' outright, and the ±70% band belongs to SPECIAL FORCES count. CIVILIAN — 10% factory counts ±50% [correct]; 40% exact factory counts [correct]; but fuel reserves ±50% is at 20%, not 50%; 70% active focus + progress [correct]. NAVY — report's ladder is substantially wrong: wiki gives 5% deployed naval manpower ±50%; 10% total fleet count (±50%) and deployed ship types; 30% task force count ±50% and ship types+counts ±50%; 40% assigned missions; 70% exact task force/ship types; 80% EXACT total fleet count plus missions and task-force counts. There is no 20% = exact fleet count. CRITICAL CORRECTION TO THE FRAMING: the claim that the estimate is 'ALWAYS SHOWN — wrong-but-bounded, never hidden' is REFUTED. At 0% intel most fields render as literal '???' — the game does hide, and only switches to bounded-estimate presentation once a threshold is crossed. MERIDIAN should note the real pattern is three-state (hidden → bounded estimate → exact), not two-state.

claim: Combat Mission execution phase 'always lasts 60 seconds of real-time', player 'cannot influence the result and is limited to watch, replay and move the camera'; unidentified contacts render as a grey symbol with a red question mark and the text 'sound'.
reasoning: The WEGO half is confirmed almost word-for-word: 'turns are divided into a planning and an executing phase. While the planning phase can, in single player mode, last as long as the player needs... the executing phase always lasts 60 seconds of real-time' and 'During the execution phase, units carry out their orders, but the player cannot influence the result and is limited to watch, replay and move the camera.' The sound-contact symbol description I could NOT verify — the Combat Mission fandom wiki returned HTTP 402 on every attempt and no alternative source was reachable within the remaining budget.
correction: Two caveats. (1) The 60-second figure applies to WEGO mode only; modern Combat Mission titles also ship a real-time mode where no such fixed phase exists, so 'the execution phase always lasts 60 seconds' is true of a mode, not of the game. State the mode explicitly. (2) The 'grey symbol / red question mark / text "sound"' rendering is UNVERIFIED — it is plausible and matches the known sound-contact mechanic, but the cited wiki was unreachable and I found no independent confirmation. To settle it: obtain the Combat Mission game manual PDF from Battlefront, or a captioned in-game screenshot of a sound contact. Do not put the specific colours and glyph into a design document as sourced fact until that is done — the transferable pattern ('unknown rendered as a typed, present object rather than absence') is safe; the exact trade dress is both unverified and something MERIDIAN is forbidden to copy anyway.

claim: Ghoniem, Fekete & Castagliola (2004): matrix beats node-link above ~20 vertices, node-link retains advantage only for path-finding.
reasoning: The paper exists — Semantic Scholar confirms the title 'A Comparison of the Readability of Graphs Using Node-Link and Matrix-Based Representations' by Ghoniem, Fekete and Castagliola — but I could not retrieve any full text or abstract to verify the two load-bearing specifics. The cited HAL page (hal.science/hal-00343819v1) is behind an Anubis anti-bot wall and returns 403; the author's own PDF mirror (lri.fr/aviz.fr), UBC, UMD, ResearchGate, IEEE Xplore and the Sage/Palgrave journal version all returned 403/404/empty. The original researcher openly states they read only an API-fetched abstract and never the paper. So the twenty-vertex figure and the path-finding exception are, at present, sourced to nothing I could read. The claim is plausible and matches the paper's reputation in the InfoVis literature, but 'matches reputation' is not verification.
correction: Do not quote 'twenty vertices' as a design threshold. What can be honestly asserted today: a well-known 2004 controlled experiment compared node-link and matrix representations and reported task-dependent results. Before the number enters a design document, someone must read the actual results section and record (a) the exact graph sizes tested, (b) the density conditions — the finding is widely understood to be density-sensitive, not purely size-sensitive, and a bare vertex count may misstate it, and (c) which task list was used and how 'path finding' was operationalised. If MERIDIAN wants a numeric node budget, derive it from MERIDIAN's own rendering and legibility testing and cite that, not this paper.
source: https://www.semanticscholar.org/search?q=A%20Comparison%20of%20the%20Readability%20of%20Graphs%20Using%20Node-Link%20and%20Matrix-Based%20Representations (title/authorship only); https://hal.science/hal-00343819v1 (403, Anubis); IEEE 1382912, Sage 10.1057/palgrave.ivs.9500092, aviz.fr and cs.ubc.ca PDF mirrors all unretrievable

claim: i2 Analyst's Notebook offers exactly four centrality measures (betweenness, closeness, degree, eigenvector), a 'Use Link Directions' checkbox, and two normalisation modes (divide-by-theoretical-maximum vs set-observed-maximum-to-100%).
reasoning: The mechanism is real and the quoted wording is accurate. setting_centrality_measures.html confirms four tickable measures (Betweenness, Closeness, Degree, Eigenvector, at least one required), confirms the 'Use Link Directions' checkbox ('the system assumes bidirectional information flow if arrows aren't displayed'), confirms the Enhanced Analysis toggle is on by default, and confirms both normalisation modes almost verbatim: standard divides by 'what, in theory, is the highest possible result for each centrality measure' and expresses percentages, while 'relative to maximum' takes 'the highest result or actual maximum' and displays it 'as 100% in the Results table'. The word 'exactly' is what fails. The parent page sna_centrality.html documents eight items under centrality, including Link betweenness ('measures the number of paths that pass through each link') and K-Core, plus link direction and link weightings as modifiers. So four is the count in the Options-tab checkbox group, not the count of centrality concepts i2 ships.
correction: i2's Social Network Analysis Options tab exposes four selectable ENTITY centrality measures — betweenness, closeness, degree, eigenvector — alongside a 'Use Link Directions' checkbox, an Enhanced Analysis toggle (default on), and two normalisation modes. i2's centrality documentation additionally covers link betweenness and K-Core, which are not in that four-checkbox group. The downstream MERIDIAN recommendation (always display measure name plus normalisation basis) survives intact — it rests on the two-mode normalisation design, which is fully verified.

claim: Action Types validate submission criteria and authorisation BEFORE commit, and fire declared side effects (notifications, webhooks, automatic link creation) AFTER commit.
reasoning: The before-commit half is independently confirmed on the submission-criteria page: submission criteria are 'the conditions that determine whether an action can be submitted', 'Actions can only be submitted if all the submission criteria are met', and the airline example evaluates state 'at the moment that the action is submitted' — a gate before execution. The after-commit half is NOT verified. The action-types overview names notification and webhook side effects and automatic link-creation rules, but does not state execution ordering relative to commit, and the dedicated side-effects sub-page returned 404 at the URL I tried. The strict two-phase 'validate → commit → side effects' sequence is the researcher's inference presented as documented fact.
correction: Documented: submission criteria (including authorisation conditions) gate whether an action can be submitted at all; side effects of type notification and webhook exist. NOT documented at the pages checked: that side effects fire strictly after commit. Treat the ordering as unverified inference. To settle it, locate the live side-effects/rules sub-page or the Actions API execution-semantics reference.

claim: Data Lineage is a standalone Foundry application carrying staleness, health, permission-checking, rollback and scheduling on the same graph as topology.
reasoning: The feature names are confirmed as documented: 'Understand out-of-date datasets' (staleness), 'Check resource permissions', dataset and pipeline rollback, 'Manage schedules', 'Branching data lineage', 'Node coloring', 'Graph elements reference', build timeline. The word 'standalone' is the weak point: Data Lineage is documented as a sub-application under Foundry's data-integration Applications section, not as an independent product. The researcher's own caveat is correct and should be preserved — feature names are documented, but visual design, node vocabulary and interaction are unverified because sub-pages render client-side. Note the design recommendation rests on features being *named*, not on any evidence they are usable or well-received; no user-experience source was produced.
correction: Say 'a dedicated Data Lineage application within Foundry' rather than 'standalone'. And state plainly that the claim is a documentation-inventory claim, not a claim about the interface.

claim: CS2 ships 33 info views, opened from a single advisor icon top-left, with terrain/roads/buildings/vehicles recoloured by DIFFERENT variables within one view (Fire & Rescue: roads by coverage, buildings by fire hazard).
reasoning: The CS2 wiki Info views page confirms the count ('33 info views'), the opening affordance ('clicking the advisor icon in the top-left corner'), and per-channel separation verbatim: Fire & Rescue roads 'green-red showing current fire & rescue coverage' vs buildings/trees 'green-red showing current fire hazard'; Police roads coverage vs buildings crime probability; Air Pollution terrain 'blue-red' pollution vs buildings/roads 'white-orange showing their generated air pollution'. The core mechanism is real. But the wiki text I retrieved recolours terrain, roads, buildings and TREES — it does not document VEHICLES as a recoloured channel in these views, and I found no documentation of wind-direction arrows in Air Pollution or water-flow arrows in Water Pollution. Also the report's Land Value description is incomplete: the wiki gives buildings 'green-red' AND terrain 'blue-white', not just blue-to-white.
correction: Say: 33 info views, advisor icon top-left, with terrain, roads, buildings and trees recoloured by different variables within a single view. Drop 'vehicles' and drop the directional-arrow claim unless confirmed by direct play — neither is documented. Land Value uses two different encodings simultaneously (buildings green-red, terrain blue-white), which is actually a STRONGER example of channel separation than the report gives.

claim: Mechanism-list scan: Democracy 4's interface is a causal web of icons with animated arrows whose thickness/speed indicates strength, and the sim is a 'custom-built neural network'; Tropico 6 has an Almanac and eight named factions with individually-simulated leaders.
reasoning: The neural-network quote is real and verbatim from the publisher's own store description: 'a custom-built neural network designed to model the opinions, beliefs, thoughts and biases of thousands of virtual citizens'. The dynamic-resizing claim is also verbatim from Positech's own site: 'Icons resize on the main screen to show popularity / finances / influence or values. Policy areas resize dynamively [sic].' However, neither source I could reach documents the ARROWS — thickness/speed encoding influence strength, or backward chain-tracing. That is the single most load-bearing Democracy 4 claim in the report and it is currently unsourced. Separately, I could not reach any source for the Tropico 6 Almanac tab structure or the eight-faction list with bribable/imprisonable named leaders (wiki host unreachable / paywalled), so that entire entry is unverified in this pass.
correction: Keep the neural-network quote and the icon-resizing claim (both verbatim-sourced). Downgrade the animated-arrow causal-web description to unverified pending a screenshot, dev blog or manual — it is the mechanism the report leans on hardest for D4 and it is the least evidenced. Treat the whole Tropico 6 entry as unverified until a reachable source is found.

claim: 1. Every alert family ships as a matched label/description pair (LowFood+LowFoodDesc etc.), an architectural rule across ~59 families
reasoning: I pulled Core/Keyed/Alerts.xml from Ludeon's own translation repo (Ludeon/RimWorld-ChineseSimplified, Core/Keyed/Alerts.xml — key names are English, values translated). 138 unique keys, 60 of them ending in 'Desc'. All four named probes exist as pairs (LowFood/LowFoodDesc, NeedDoctor/NeedDoctorDesc, NeedColonistBeds/NeedColonistBedsDesc, AlertTatteredApparel/AlertTatteredApparelDesc, FireInHomeArea/FireInHomeAreaDesc). But it is NOT universal: NeedWarmClothes, AlertAnimalPenNeededNoHandlers and AlertTimedRaidsArrivingAt have no matching Desc key at all, and several singular variants (ColonistIdle, ColonistNeedsRescue, ActivatorCountdownMultiple) deliberately share the other variant's Desc. Also this is Core only — Anomaly/Biotech ship their own Keyed/Alerts.xml, and many alerts build their explanation in C# (Alert.GetExplanation is virtual and frequently overridden), so the string table is not the whole population.
source: https://github.com/Ludeon/RimWorld-ChineseSimplified — Core/Keyed/Alerts.xml (fetched via GitHub API); Alert.cs in https://github.com/Chillu1/RimWorldDecompiled
correction: Label+description is the dominant pattern, not an exceptionless rule: in Core, ~60 of ~65 alert families have a keyed Desc; a few have none, and singular/plural variants share one Desc. Some descriptions are generated in code rather than keyed. Cite it as 'near-universal convention', not 'every alert'.

claim: 2. Universal stat drill-down with sections base → traits → gear → health conditions → skills → other/post-process → post-process curve → final, with typed multipliers and offsets
reasoning: The mechanism is real and stronger than claimed, but the stated ORDER and section list are wrong. Core/Keyed/Dialog_StatsReports.xml contains StatsReport_BaseValue, _RelevantTraits, _RelevantGear, _RelevantHediffs, _Skills, _Health/_HealthFactors, _OtherStats, _PostProcessed, _FinalValue, _MaxValue, plus many the report omits: _Ideoligion, _Genes, _LifeStage, _Material, _QualityMultiplier/_QualityOffset, _AgeMultiplier/_AgeOffset, _Inspiration, _ScenarioFactor, _DifficultyMultiplier, _StuffEffectMultiplier, _InBed, StatReport_PossibleOffsets, Stat_Thing_Weapon_Range_Clamped/_Multiplier. Reading StatWorker.GetExplanationUnfinalized/GetExplanationFinalizePart in the decompile: base value first, then a set of contributors whose emission order is skills/health/traits/gear/hediffs/ideo/genes/lifestage/material (traits are NOT second), then the post-process CURVE line (StatsReport_PostProcessed, rendered as 'x => y'), then StatsReport_OtherStats, then ScenarioFactor, then FinalValue and optionally MaxValue. So 'other factors' comes AFTER the post-process curve, and the report's 'other factors (post-process)' + 'post-process curve' are two different things it has conflated and inverted. Typed offsets vs factors is confirmed (ToStringNumberSense.Offset / .Factor throughout).
correction: Correct order: base value → contributor blocks (skills, health, traits, gear, hediffs, ideoligion, genes, life stage, material/stuff, quality, age, inspiration — order varies by stat and requester) → post-process curve line (StatsReport_PostProcessed, 'before => after') → other stats → scenario/difficulty factor → final value (→ max value / clamping). Do not present the eight-step sequence as canonical.

claim: 4. Thought definitions carry both a label and a labelSocial per stage, so the same fact is worded differently in the mood ledger vs another pawn's opinion breakdown
reasoning: This is the weakest claim in the report and the reviewer marked it VERIFIED, which it is not. labelSocial is a real ThoughtDef stage field, but it is rare, not per-stage-standard. In Core/DefInjected/ThoughtDef/Thoughts_Memory_Social.xml there are 51 stage .label entries and only 3 labelSocial entries (SoldMyBondedAnimal, SoldMyBondedAnimalMood, SoldMyLovedOne). Thoughts_Situation_Social.xml (38 labels), Thoughts_Memory_Misc.xml (48), Thoughts_Situation_Traits.xml (43) and Thoughts_Situation_General.xml (29) contain ZERO labelSocial entries. DefInjected files are generated from the translatable fields actually present on the defs, so absence means the field is unset. RimWorld's actual mechanism for divergent wording is a paired-def split: e.g. CheatedOnMe (social thought) and CheatedOnMeMood (mood thought) are two separate defs with different stage names (cheated_on_me vs cheated_on_by) and only the mood one carries a description.
source: Ludeon/RimWorld-ChineseSimplified, Core/DefInjected/ThoughtDef/*.xml (Thoughts_Memory_Social, Thoughts_Situation_Social, Thoughts_Memory_Misc, Thoughts_Situation_Traits, Thoughts_Situation_General)
correction: labelSocial exists as an optional per-stage override used on a small minority of thoughts. The general shipped precedent for 'same fact, different panel, different wording' is a SPLIT DEF pattern (a social ThoughtDef plus a companion *Mood ThoughtDef, differently worded and separately staged), not a universal dual-label field. MERIDIAN's four-views requirement can still cite RimWorld, but must cite the split-def pattern.

claim: ASKB: Bloomberg's generative-AI research tool is invoked as 'ASKB <GO>' in the command line, and 'every response links back to the original document'.
reasoning: I could not obtain a single independent corroborating source. bloomberg.com returns HTTP 403 to automated fetches (both the press-release URL and the Terminal AI product page), so Bloomberg's own wording is inaccessible to me, and web search was exhausted. The claim therefore still rests entirely on one practitioner LinkedIn post. Two distinct sub-claims are at risk and should be separated: (a) the mnemonic string 'ASKB', and (b) per-response source linking. Neither is attested anywhere I could reach. Mnemonics are exactly the kind of detail a secondary write-up gets plausibly-but-wrongly (cf. the report's own 'HISTORY key' problem below), and 'links back to the original document' is a marketing-shaped sentence that a practitioner could restate from a demo without it being a per-response guarantee.
source: bloomberg.com/company/press/... and bloomberg.com/professional/products/bloomberg-terminal/ai/ — both HTTP 403, not retrievable
correction: Do not cite the mnemonic or the per-response source-linking guarantee in the design document. State only what Bloomberg's press release actually supports: a natural-language interface for questioning documents within Bloomberg's own data universe. If the provenance rule needs commercial validation, it must come from a hands-on Terminal session or Bloomberg's own help page (HELP ASKB), not from this report.

claim: Launchpad components are joined into explicitly named security groups via a Group Manager; selection in a Monitor propagates to linked Chart and News; 'Show on Selected Pages' (shared instance) is distinct from 'Duplicate to Page' (independent copy).
reasoning: The original readkong-hosted Bloomberg guide is now HTTP 410 Gone, so the report's sole substantive source is dead and I could not re-read it. Penn's Bloomberg guide describes Launchpad only as 'a tool used to personalize your Bloomberg desktop' and links to a post titled 'Adding Components and Pages and Organizing the Group View' — that phrase corroborates the existence of grouping vocabulary and nothing about the mechanism. Crucially, none of the load-bearing specifics (a 'Group Manager' dialog, auto-named 'Group-1', the red-highlight-then-Update sequence, Settings > Add to Security Group, Link To > Component Groups, or the Show-on-Selected-Pages / Duplicate-to-Page distinction) are attested by any source I could reach.
source: https://guides.library.upenn.edu/bloomberg/launchpad (vocabulary only); readkong source now HTTP 410 Gone
correction: Treat the explicit-named-groups mechanism as an unverified hypothesis. The transferable pattern for MERIDIAN (explicit, user-named follow-groups plus pinning, versus implicit global propagation) is a defensible design position on its own merits — but it must be argued as MERIDIAN's own design inference, not presented as 'Bloomberg does this'. A dead single source cannot underwrite the report's highest-value recommendation.

claim: Colour encodes interaction affordance under a closed, exceptionless legend: amber/yellow = editable field, white-outlined box = clickable list item, red bar = function panel header.
reasoning: Colour-as-semantics is real at the keyboard layer and I confirmed part of it independently: Wikipedia's Bloomberg Terminal article states the keys are colour-coded — red Cancel, green GO, yellow market-sector hotkeys. That establishes colour carries meaning in the product. It does NOT establish the on-screen legend. I could not reach the Imperial guide (404) and the Penn guide contains no colour conventions at all. More importantly, no source I found asserts the legend is CLOSED or EXCEPTIONLESS — that universality is the report's inference, and it is the part doing all the argumentative work. A guide describing the common case is entirely compatible with inconsistent application across thousands of functions.
source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bloomberg_Terminal (keyboard colour coding confirmed); Imperial guide 404; Penn guide silent on colour
correction: Accurate version: Bloomberg uses colour semantically, confirmed at the keyboard layer (green GO, red Cancel, yellow sector keys) and reported by library guides for on-screen elements. The claim that the on-screen legend is closed, exhaustive and consistently applied across all functions is unverified and should be dropped. The 'expert density is legitimate because it is parseable on first sight' thesis needs a weaker formulation — colour reduces parsing cost where applied — or independent evidence of consistency.

claim: PDFU COLORS <GO> offers deuteranopia and protanomaly schemes, 'supported by a limited number of functions at this time', with Buy/Sell buttons not yet compatible.
reasoning: The single cited source (John Cabot University library guide) is not reachable — library.jcu.edu fails DNS resolution (ENOTFOUND) — so I could not even confirm the source says what the report claims it says, let alone that it is current. The report itself flags the staleness risk correctly. The specific 'Buy/Sell buttons not compatible' detail is the sort of caveat that is both highly plausible and highly perishable.
source: library.jcu.edu — DNS resolution failure, source not retrievable
correction: Do not use this as a factual cautionary example about Bloomberg. The sequencing argument (DESIGN-TOKENS before SCREEN-SPECIFICATIONS) is sound independently: if colour carries semantic load and an alternate palette is retrofitted later, coverage gaps are the predictable failure mode. Make that argument from first principles and drop the unverified Bloomberg specifics, or verify via Bloomberg's own PDFU documentation first.

claim: Mechanism-list scan: keyboard/panel/sector-key details, and the suspect items within them.
reasoning: Verified correct: the four-Panel model, each Panel containing 'a separate instance of the terminal command line', is stated verbatim by Wikipedia. The yellow sector-key mappings check out precisely — Wikipedia lists F2-F12 as GOVT, CORP, MTGE, M-Mkt, MUNI, PFD, EQUITY, COMDTY, INDEX, CURNCY, which places EQUITY at F8, COMDTY at F9 and CURNCY at F11 exactly as the report states. Suspect items I flag for the parent agent: (1) a keyboard key labelled HISTORY that repopulates the command line — Wikipedia's key enumeration (MENU, GO, CANCEL, MESSAGE) does not include it, and this looks conflated with the LAST <GO> FUNCTION, which the report separately and correctly lists as a mnemonic; asserting both a HISTORY key and a LAST function risks double-counting one feature. (2) The Component Browser opening with 'the 25 most popular components' with star ratings — a version-specific UI detail almost certainly drawn from a dated guide and likely stale. (3) Monitor limits of 2,000 securities / 30 columns / 'more than 280,000 data items' — suspiciously precise, single-sourced, and the kind of figure that drifts between product versions. (4) The GO key's green deriving from the Monopoly board is Bloomberg folklore; the report hedges it with 'reportedly', which is the right treatment but it should not appear in a design document as fact.
correction: Keep the sector-key mappings and the four-Panel model — both independently confirmed. Strike or explicitly mark as unverified: the HISTORY key (probably a conflation with LAST <GO>), the '25 most popular components' browser layout, the Monitor 2,000/30/280,000 figures, and the Monopoly anecdote. None of these change MERIDIAN's design conclusions, but they are exactly the class of unverified specific this project must not propagate.

claim: Security Copilot's Defender incident summary is a distinct card in a separate Copilot pane with its own Cancel/Regenerate/feedback/See-prompts controls, and generation is a configurable policy (Always / minimum severity / on demand) shown with estimated per-day incident counts and SCU cost.
reasoning: The policy half is fully confirmed, including the cost disclosure: 'Always (for every incident opened) / Based on the severity level of the incident / On demand only', and 'When you select Incident severity, an estimate of the number of incidents of each severity level reviewed per day is displayed, along with the estimated SCU consumption.' The container half is imprecise about where controls live. Structural provenance survives as a recommendation; the specific control inventory does not.
correction: Per the doc, generation begins in the Tasks pane — 'Copilot automatically creates an incident summary in the Tasks pane. You can stop the summary creation by selecting Cancel or restart creation by selecting Regenerate' — and only then 'the incident summary card loads on the Copilot pane.' Regenerate/copy/'Open in Security Copilot' appear under a More actions (...) ellipsis at the top of the card; 'See prompts' is on the card; the feedback icon is 'found on the bottom of the Copilot pane', not on the card. Also, the settings surface is documented as preview and reached via Microsoft Sentinel's nav (System > Settings > Copilot in Defender), not from the Defender incident page.

claim: Attack Discovery analyses by default up to 100 alerts from the last 24 hours, and Copilot summarises incidents containing up to 100 alerts — AI narratives here operate over a capped sample; whether the cap is disclosed in-UI is unverified.
reasoning: Both numbers are real but they are two different kinds of limit, and the report's own 'disclosure unverified' hedge is now partly falsifiable in its favour.
correction: (a) Attack Discovery: confirmed verbatim — 'By default, Attack Discovery analyzes up to 100 alerts from the last 24 hours, but you can customize how many and which alerts it analyzes using the settings menu.' The cap IS surfaced in-UI: the settings menu exposes a 'Number of alerts' slider, so for this product the 'unverified' hedge should be upgraded to disclosed-and-adjustable. (b) Copilot: the doc says 'Incidents containing up to 100 alerts can be summarized into one incident summary' — that is a stated capacity ceiling on what can be summarised, not a documented sampling of a larger population, and nothing on the page says what happens above 100 or that the analyst is told. Do not present the two as the same mechanism. The design recommendation to render coverage as engine-computed fact still stands, but its strongest support is Attack Discovery's slider, not Copilot.

claim: Mechanism-list scan: the quoted phrase 'a panelized version of the entity page', the Actions-menu contents, and the three-panel entity page / mandatory-classification items.
reasoning: Most of the Sentinel mechanism list checks out verbatim against the live doc — 'an aggregation of all the relevant evidence', 'up to 20' similar incidents with a 'Similarity reason' column and info-icon hover revealing 'entities, rule name, or details', the full Top insights list, the stated Top-insights window '24 hours before the earliest alert in the incident until the time of the latest alert', the in-page Logs panel with auto-run query and 'Done', and checkbox-to-bookmark with 'Add bookmark to the current incident' vs 'Add bookmark'. The Alertmanager entry also checks out, including 'hundreds to thousands of alerts may be firing simultaneously', 'a single compact notification', inhibition 'configured through the Alertmanager's configuration file' and 'Silences are configured in the web interface of the Alertmanager'. Three items need correction or carry no verification.
correction: (1) SUSPECT QUOTE: 'described in the docs as a panelized version of the entity page' does not appear anywhere on the investigate-incidents page, which instead describes an Entities-tab side pane with Info / Timeline / Insights cards. Treat as unverified and do not quote it. (2) CONFLATION: the report's mechanism 1 lists Investigate inside the incident Actions menu; the doc states the opposite for the incident page — 'The only one missing is Investigate, which is available on the left-hand details panel instead.' (3) NOT CHECKED, do not treat as established: the three-part entity page with its named source-system lists and supported entity types (a different doc, entity-pages); the claim that Sentinel close-classification is mandatory and the Elastic workflow_reason taxonomy; the Grafana Alerting defaults (30s group wait / 5m group interval / 4h repeat interval) and the '100 phone calls' quote; and the Elastic 'Group alerts by' three-field limit and 'Event rendered view'.

claim: ICD 203 mandates seven likelihood bands with the exact ranges given, and explicitly forbids combining a confidence level and a degree of likelihood in the same sentence.
reasoning: I could not retrieve ICD 203 from any host this session: dni.gov, odni.gov, intelligence.gov and cia.gov returned HTTP 403, irp.fas.org returned empty extraction, and curl was blocked by the sandbox (HTTP 202, 0 bytes). Wikipedia's 'Words of estimative probability' article does not reference ICD 203 at all, so it offers no corroboration. Critically, the second source the report cites as independent verification — docslib.org — fetched successfully but returned a wine retail price list, containing no likelihood table and no confidence/likelihood sentence. So the claimed two-source verification is not reproducible: one source was unreachable and the other does not contain the material. The band values are consistent with my background knowledge of the 2015 ICD 203, but background knowledge is exactly what this exercise forbids substituting for a source, and the no-blending prohibition is the load-bearing part and the part I have least independent trace of.
source: docslib.org/doc/1129399/... (fetched: unrelated content); dni.gov / odni.gov / intelligence.gov / cia.gov ICD 203 PDFs (403); irp.fas.org/dni/icd/icd-203.pdf (empty); en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Words_of_estimative_probability (no ICD 203 reference)
correction: Do not cite docslib.org as corroboration — that URL does not host ICD 203. Before this claim enters a design document, fetch the ICD 203 PDF directly (or a library/university mirror) and quote the uncertainty section verbatim, paying particular attention to whether the no-blending text is phrased as a prohibition ('must not') or as weaker guidance, since the two-slot UI rule depends on the strong reading.

claim: PHIA yardstick has seven deliberately non-contiguous bands with the stated gaps, and PHIA separates probability from analytical confidence (High/Moderate/Low) against information base, analytical rigour, and complexity & volatility.
reasoning: The gov.uk page confirms almost all of this from the primary source. Bands verbatim: '>0% - ~5% Remote Chance', '~10% - ~20% Highly Unlikely', '~25% - ~35% Unlikely', '~40% - <50% Realistic Possibility', '~55% - ~75% Likely or Probable', '~80% - ~90% Highly Likely', '~95% - <100% Almost Certain' — so the gaps are real in the source text, not a rendering artefact. The three confidence factors are confirmed and named exactly as claimed. The probability/confidence distinction is confirmed near-verbatim: 'Probability reflects the likelihood that a statement is true, [while] analytical confidence reflects the soundness and stability of the foundations on which the assessment of likelihood has been made.' The reason-for-words claim is confirmed: terms are used 'to avoid interpretation of judgements as being overly precise, as most intelligence judgements are not based on quantitative data.' The one unsupported word is 'deliberately'. The document does not state that the inter-band gaps are an intentional design choice; it says only that 'the choice of terms and ranges was informed by academic research and they align as closely as possible with an average reader's understanding.' Intentionality is the researcher's inference, and the hysteresis proposal rests on it.
correction: State that the bands are non-contiguous as published (verified), but label the 'deliberate' reading as design inference, not PHIA doctrine. The gaps may equally be a byproduct of rounding bands to reader-intuitive endpoints. A gapped-band UI is still defensible, but argue it on its own merits rather than on borrowed PHIA intent.

claim: Baker et al. (1968) found ~87% of Admiralty-code ratings fell on the diagonal (A1, B2, C3...).
reasoning: The report already concedes this is second-hand from a Blockint critical review rather than the 1968 original. I could not reach even the second-hand source: the Blockint Admiralty-system review URL returned HTTP 404, and with the search budget exhausted I could not locate an alternative host or the original Baker paper. So the statistic currently has zero retrievable support in this session — neither primary nor secondary. Given that this single number is described as carrying the entire warning against two-axis grading that covaries, it is the highest-risk item in the set.
correction: Treat the 87% figure as unsourced until the Baker, Mckendry & Mace (1968) original — or a peer-reviewed paper quoting it, e.g. the Irwin/Mandel literature on source-reliability covariance — is obtained and the exact figure, sample and definition of 'diagonal' are read. The underlying design warning (nominally independent axes tend to covary in practice) is well-supported in the wider literature and can be made without this specific statistic.

claim: ICD 206 requires source descriptors addressing denial/deception among named factors, and encourages a source summary statement identifying which sources are most important and which corroborate or conflict.
reasoning: Every host I tried for ICD 206 failed: intelligence.gov and dni.gov returned 403, irp.fas.org/dni/icd/icd-206.pdf returned empty extraction, and curl was sandbox-blocked. I have no retrieved text supporting either half of this claim. The content is plausible and matches my general understanding of ICD 206, but I have no source, and this claim underwrites two separate recommended UI features (the deception flag as its own field, and the evidential-spine panel), so it should not pass on plausibility.
correction: Fetch ICD 206 directly and verify two things separately: (a) that denial/deception is a named enumerated factor rather than subsumed in a general reliability judgement, and (b) whether the source summary statement is 'required', 'encouraged', or merely 'recommended for complex products' — the report says 'encourages', which is weaker than 'requires' and would not by itself justify making the evidential-spine panel mandatory in the UI.

claim: Friedman and Zeckhauser found the 2007 Iran NIE used 'confidence' 19 times in its Key Judgements to express probability, never separately stating both dimensions.
reasoning: The Dartmouth-hosted PDF URL the report cites resolved to HTTP 404, and the ResearchGate record for 'Assessing uncertainty in intelligence' returned HTTP 403. With no search budget I could not locate another copy of the Intelligence and National Security article. The claim is specific enough (a count of 19) to be checkable, but I have checked nothing. Note also a latent risk in the claim's second half: 'never separately stating both dimensions' is a strong universal, and papers of this kind more often report that the NIE conflated the two in most instances rather than in every instance.
correction: Re-locate the Friedman & Zeckhauser article (Intelligence and National Security) and verify both the count of 19 and whether the authors' claim is universal ('never') or predominant ('largely'). If it is the weaker form, the argument for structural enforcement over guidance still holds but should be stated as such.

claim: TCAS thresholds: TA within 40 seconds of potential collision, RA within 25 seconds; pilots shall not manoeuvre in response to a TA alone
correction: The procedural rule is confirmed and should be used as stated: pilots 'shall not manoeuvre their aircraft in response to traffic advisories (TAs) only' — the TA primes attention and supports visual acquisition — whereas an RA requires immediate response. The TIMING figures, however, should not be written into a design document as fixed constants. 40s/25s are widely-cited NOMINAL values; in TCAS II the actual tau alert thresholds are set by Sensitivity Level, which varies with own-aircraft altitude, so both TA and RA warning times are shorter at low altitude and longer at high altitude. State them as 'approximately 40s / 25s at typical cruise sensitivity levels' or cite the SL table, not as absolutes.
source: Wikipedia, Traffic collision avoidance system, https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Traffic_collision_avoidance_system (secondary). Primary sources FAA AC 120-55D and SKYbrary both returned HTTP 403 this session — the SL/tau table remains unverified against primary text.
reasoning: The fetched source confirms TA 'within 40 seconds of potential collision', RA 'within 25 seconds of potential collision', the 'Traffic, traffic' and 'Climb, climb'/'Descend, descend' annunciations, and the shall-not-manoeuvre rule verbatim. What it does NOT support is the implication of fixed thresholds: it states only that the protection volume 'depends on the altitude, speed, and heading of the aircraft involved' and contains no SL table. Since MERIDIAN's structural ban on acting from the awareness tier rests on the PROCEDURAL rule and not on the numbers, the design pattern is safe; only the numeric precision needs softening. Caveat: this is a secondary source. The 'shall not manoeuvre' wording originates in ICAO PANS-OPS/Annex 2 doctrine — verify against ICAO Doc 8168 before quoting it as regulatory text.

claim: Colour-as-exception / grey-scale-first process display rule attributed to ASM Consortium and high-performance HMI literature
correction: Do not attribute this rule to ASM or ISA-101 in VISUAL-CONSTITUTION on current evidence. Either (a) obtain ASM's 'Effective Operator Display Design' guidelines and the purchased ISA-101 text and quote them directly, or (b) present the rule as MERIDIAN's own design decision, argued on its merits, with the dark-cockpit analogy clearly labelled as design inference rather than cited canon.

(3) ECAM: the three-level structure with differentiated attention-getters matches Airbus FCOM doctrine as I understand it, but 'Level 1' terminology varies between Airbus documentation and secondary sources (some reserve Level 1 for advisories/status). Verify against an actual FCOM before asserting the level numbering.
